---
title: "Bayesian IRT Analysis of INVALSI Test Data"
author: "Riccardo Pugliese"
date: "`r Sys.Date()`"
output:
  html_document:
    toc: true
    toc_float: true
    number_sections: true
    theme: flatly
---

```{r setup, include=FALSE}
knitr::opts_chunk$set(
  echo = TRUE,
  warning = FALSE,
  message = FALSE,
  fig.width = 8,
  fig.height = 5
)
```
# Introduction

This project applies Bayesian Item Response Theory to INVALSI Grade 10 Italian test data.

The data used in this project come from the official INVALSI statistical service page:

<https://serviziostatistico.invalsi.it/en/invalsi_ss_data/microdati-campione-g10-2015-16-italiano/>

The dataset is called:

**Microdati Campione G10 2015-16 Italiano**

These data are official INVALSI sample microdata for the Grade 10 Italian test (second year of high school, 15/16 years old). They are student-level data, meaning that each row refers to one student. The dataset has already been prepared, structured, and cleaned by INVALSI before being released. Therefore, it is not a raw dataset collected directly from schools, but an official dataset made available for statistical and research purposes.

The dataset is also based on a balanced sample. For this project, this aspect is not the main methodological focus, because the goal is not to estimate national descriptive statistics. However, it is still useful to know that the data come from an official and carefully designed INVALSI sample, rather than from an arbitrary collection of students.

The actual Italian test questions given to students can be found here:

<https://www.engheben.it/prof/materiali/invalsi/invalsi_seconda_superiore/2015-2016/invalsi_italiano_2015-2016_secondaria_seconda.pdf>

This is useful because it allows us to connect the item columns in the dataset with the real questions answered by the students.

Item Response Theory is useful because it does not only consider the final score of a student. Instead, it analyses the full pattern of correct and incorrect answers. In this way, the model can estimate latent characteristics of both students and questions.

The main goal of the project is to estimate two latent quantities:

- `theta[i]`: the ability of student `i`;
- `b[j]`: the difficulty of item `j`.

These quantities are called latent because they are not directly observed in the dataset. What we observe is only whether each student answered each question correctly or incorrectly. Bayesian modelling allows us to estimate these latent quantities through posterior distributions.

The project compares two Bayesian IRT models:

1. Rasch / 1PL model;
2. 2PL model.

The Rasch model estimates student ability and item difficulty. The 2PL model extends the Rasch model by adding item discrimination, which measures how well an item separates stronger and weaker students.

I chose the 2015/16 dataset instead of more recent INVALSI datasets for an important methodological reason. In more recent years, INVALSI tests have increasingly moved to computer-based testing. In this setting, students may not all receive exactly the same set of questions. Even if the tests are designed to be comparable, this creates a more complex item-response structure, with many missing responses by design.

The simple Rasch and 2PL models used in this project assume a clean response matrix, where the same item columns correspond to the same questions for all students. If students answer different sets of items, the model becomes more complicated and requires a more advanced IRT structure.

For this reason, the 2015/16 Grade 10 Italian dataset is more appropriate for this project. It provides real educational data, binary item responses, and an official external score, while keeping the response matrix simple enough for a Bayesian MCMC analysis in JAGS.

The official score `WLE_ITA_200` is not used inside the Bayesian models. It is used only later as an external validation benchmark. If the Bayesian estimate of student ability is meaningful, it should be positively related to the official INVALSI WLE score.

# Dataset structure

The dataset contains several different types of variables.

First, it contains student-level information. These variables describe some characteristics of the students included in the sample.

Second, it contains school-related information. These variables refer to the school context of each student, for example information connected to the school or class.

Third, the dataset includes performance-related variables, such as official INVALSI scores. In particular, the variable `WLE_ITA_200` represents the official INVALSI ability score for the Italian test, expressed on the INVALSI scale.

The dataset also contain information about students' results in other INVALSI tests or other assessment-related variables. Therefore, the original file is richer than what is strictly needed for this project.

For the purpose of this analysis, however, we are interested only in two types of variables:

1. the binary item-response variables;
2. the official `WLE_ITA_200` score.

The binary item-response variables are the columns that indicate whether each student answered each Italian test item correctly or incorrectly. Each selected item column represents one Italian test question.

The answer is coded as:

- `1` = correct answer;
- `0` = incorrect answer.

Therefore, the data used in the Bayesian models can be written as a matrix \(Y\), where \(Y_{ij}\) is the answer of student \(i\) to item \(j\).

Each row of this matrix represents one Grade 10 student. Each column represents one selected Italian test item.

The Bayesian IRT models are estimated only using this binary response matrix. The models do not directly use the final score of the student. Instead, they try to reconstruct student ability and item difficulty from the observed pattern of correct and incorrect answers.

The variable `WLE_ITA_200` is kept separately. It is not used inside the Rasch model or the 2PL model. It is used only at the end of the analysis as an external validation benchmark.

The idea is simple: if the Bayesian estimate of student ability is reasonable, students with higher posterior ability estimates should also tend to have higher official INVALSI WLE scores.

# Load packages

```{r packages}
library(haven)
library(dplyr)
library(ggplot2)
library(ggrepel)
library(rjags)
library(coda)
library(tidyr)

set.seed(123)
```

# Settings

Change only the values in this section before running the project.

Here we can select three different type of running mode. If you select `"fast"` you have a model that runs fast, thanks to low number of sampled students, MCMC chain, adaptation iterations, burn-in iterations discarded before saving posterior draws and posterior iterations after burn-in.
If you use `"final"`, then basically you have a model with higher parameters.
If you use `"reuse"`, you are reloading the same saved sample, posterior results, and simulation settings without running MCMC again.


```{r settings}
# Choose one of these modes:
# "fast"  = lighter settings for testing the project quickly.
# "final" = stronger settings for the final run.
# "reuse" = do not run MCMC again; reload the previous sample, posterior results, and settings.
run_mode <- "reuse"

# Set TRUE if you want to estimate the 2PL model in fast/final mode.
# In reuse mode this value is automatically taken from run_metadata.RData.
run_2pl <- TRUE

# Set TRUE if you want to compute DIC during the first fast/final model run.
# The computed DIC is then saved and reloaded in reuse mode, so it is not recomputed every time.
run_dic <- TRUE

# Folder where the output files are saved and reloaded.
# "." means the current working directory.
results_dir <- "."

# Put here the path to the extracted INVALSI .SAV file.
# Do not put the path to the .zip file.
data_path <- "C:/Users/39392/Desktop/università/data science/tardella/progetto/Matrice_10_ita_campione_0_1_WLE_anonima.SAV"

# Stop the code if the user accidentally gives the .zip file instead of the .SAV file.
if(grepl("\\.zip$", data_path, ignore.case = TRUE)) {
  stop("You inserted the path to a .zip file. Please extract the zip file first and use the path to the .SAV file.")
}

# Helper function used later to convert a JAGS DIC object into a simple table.
extract_dic_values <- function(dic_object, model_name) {
  data.frame(
    model = model_name,
    mean_deviance = sum(dic_object$deviance),
    effective_parameters_pD = sum(dic_object$penalty),
    DIC = sum(dic_object$deviance) + sum(dic_object$penalty)
  )
}

# Helper function used later to summarize the MCMC trace of the total deviance.
# This is useful because DIC is based on the posterior mean deviance.
summarise_deviance_trace <- function(deviance_samples, model_name) {
  deviance_values <- as.numeric(as.matrix(deviance_samples)[, "total_deviance"])
  
  data.frame(
    model = model_name,
    mean_total_deviance = mean(deviance_values),
    sd_total_deviance = sd(deviance_values),
    lower_95 = as.numeric(quantile(deviance_values, 0.025)),
    median = as.numeric(quantile(deviance_values, 0.500)),
    upper_95 = as.numeric(quantile(deviance_values, 0.975)),
    monte_carlo_se = sd(deviance_values) / sqrt(length(deviance_values))
  )
}

# This function creates the project settings for fast/final mode.
# In reuse mode, it reloads the settings saved during the previous fast/final run.
configure_project_run <- function(run_mode = c("fast", "final", "reuse"),
                                  run_2pl = TRUE,
                                  run_dic = FALSE,
                                  results_dir = ".") {
  
  run_mode <- match.arg(run_mode)
  
  if(!dir.exists(results_dir)) {
    dir.create(results_dir, recursive = TRUE)
  }
  
  metadata_file <- file.path(results_dir, "run_metadata.RData")
  
  if(run_mode == "fast") {
    settings <- list(
      n_sample = 500,
      n_chains = 3,
      n_adapt = 200,
      burn_in = 200,
      n_iter = 500,
      thin_value = 1
    )
  }
  
  if(run_mode == "final") {
    settings <- list(
      n_sample = 1500,
      n_chains = 3,
      n_adapt = 1000,
      burn_in = 1000,
      n_iter = 3000,
      thin_value = 3
    )
  }
  
  if(run_mode == "reuse") {
    
    if(!file.exists(metadata_file)) {
      stop(
        "Reuse mode was selected, but run_metadata.RData was not found in results_dir. ",
        "Run the project once in fast or final mode first, so the settings can be saved."
      )
    }
    
    load(metadata_file, envir = environment())
    
    required_metadata_fields <- c(
      "run_mode", "n_sample", "n_items", "n_chains",
      "n_adapt", "burn_in", "n_iter", "thin_value", "run_2pl"
    )
    
    missing_metadata_fields <- setdiff(required_metadata_fields, names(run_metadata))
    
    if(length(missing_metadata_fields) > 0) {
      stop(
        "run_metadata.RData exists, but it is missing these fields: ",
        paste(missing_metadata_fields, collapse = ", "),
        ". Re-run the project in fast or final mode with this updated file."
      )
    }
    
    settings <- list(
      n_sample = run_metadata$n_sample,
      n_items = run_metadata$n_items,
      n_chains = run_metadata$n_chains,
      n_adapt = run_metadata$n_adapt,
      burn_in = run_metadata$burn_in,
      n_iter = run_metadata$n_iter,
      thin_value = run_metadata$thin_value
    )
    
    run_2pl <- run_metadata$run_2pl

    # In reuse mode, DIC can be shown only if it was computed and saved during a previous run.
    run_dic <- isTRUE(run_metadata$run_dic) &&
      file.exists(file.path(results_dir, "dic_results.RData"))
  }
  
  settings$run_mode <- run_mode
  settings$reuse_existing_results <- run_mode == "reuse"
  settings$original_run_mode <- if(run_mode == "reuse") run_metadata$run_mode else run_mode
  settings$run_2pl <- run_2pl
  settings$run_dic <- run_dic
  settings$results_dir <- results_dir
  settings$metadata_file <- metadata_file
  
  if(run_mode == "reuse") {
    required_files <- c(
      "run_metadata.RData",
      "sampled_data.RData",
      "rasch_results.RData"
    )
    
    if(run_2pl) {
      required_files <- c(required_files, "two_model_comparison_results.RData")
    }
    
    missing_files <- required_files[!file.exists(file.path(results_dir, required_files))]
    
    if(length(missing_files) > 0) {
      stop(
        "Reuse mode was selected, but these files are missing in results_dir: ",
        paste(missing_files, collapse = ", "),
        ". Run the project once in fast or final mode first, or move the saved files into results_dir."
      )
    }
  }
  
  settings
}

project_settings <- configure_project_run(
  run_mode = run_mode,
  run_2pl = run_2pl,
  run_dic = run_dic,
  results_dir = results_dir
)

list2env(project_settings, envir = .GlobalEnv)

cat("Project running settings:\n")
cat("These values control the sample size and the MCMC algorithm.\n\n")

cat("Selected running mode:\n")
print(run_mode)

if(reuse_existing_results) {
  cat("\nSaved results were originally created with this mode:\n")
  print(original_run_mode)
}

cat("\nEstimate 2PL model?\n")
print(run_2pl)

cat("\nCompute DIC?\n")
print(run_dic)

cat("\nNumber of sampled students used in the model:\n")
print(n_sample)

cat("\nNumber of MCMC chains:\n")
print(n_chains)

cat("\nNumber of adaptation iterations:\n")
print(n_adapt)

cat("\nNumber of burn-in iterations discarded before saving posterior draws:\n")
print(burn_in)

cat("\nNumber of posterior iterations after burn-in:\n")
print(n_iter)

cat("\nThinning value used when saving MCMC draws:\n")
print(thin_value)
```

# Load the dataset

Our dataset was saved in a `.SAV`, and in this section, the SPSS `.SAV` file is loaded into R.

The first commands are used only to inspect the dataset, in order to have an idea of which type of dataset we are dealing with.Here I check its dimensions and look at the names of all its variables.

```{r load-data}
raw_data <- read_sav(data_path)

# Function to infer the type of each variable
infer_variable_type <- function(x, var_name) {
  
  x_no_na <- x[!is.na(x)]
  n_unique <- length(unique(x_no_na))
  n_total <- length(x)
  name_low <- tolower(var_name)
  
  if (n_unique == 0) {
    return("Only missing values")
  }
  
  if (grepl("codice|cod_|id|studente|scuola|plesso|classe", name_low) &&
      n_unique > 20) {
    return("Identifier / code")
  }
  
  if (n_unique == 2) {
    return("Binary variable")
  }
  
  if (is.numeric(x) && n_unique <= 10) {
    return("Discrete numerical / ordinal variable")
  }
  
  if (is.numeric(x) && n_unique > 10) {
    return("Numerical variable")
  }
  
  if (is.character(x) || is.factor(x)) {
    return("Categorical variable")
  }
  
  if ("haven_labelled" %in% class(x)) {
    return("Labelled SPSS variable")
  }
  
  return("Other type")
}

# Function to show a few example values
get_example_values <- function(x, max_values = 6) {
  x_no_na <- x[!is.na(x)]
  
  if (length(x_no_na) == 0) {
    return(NA)
  }
  
  values <- unique(as.character(x_no_na))
  paste(head(values, max_values), collapse = ", ")
}

# Function to check whether the variable has SPSS labels
has_value_labels <- function(x) {
  if (!is.null(attr(x, "labels"))) {
    return("Yes")
  } else {
    return("No")
  }
}

# Dataset dimensions
dataset_dimensions <- data.frame(
  rows = nrow(raw_data),
  columns = ncol(raw_data)
)

dataset_dimensions

# Summary table for all variables
variable_summary <- data.frame(
  variable_name = names(raw_data),
  r_class = sapply(raw_data, function(x) paste(class(x), collapse = " / ")),
  n_unique_values = sapply(raw_data, function(x) length(unique(x[!is.na(x)]))),
  n_missing = sapply(raw_data, function(x) sum(is.na(x))),
  has_spss_labels = sapply(raw_data, has_value_labels),
  example_values = sapply(raw_data, get_example_values),
  inferred_type = mapply(infer_variable_type, raw_data, names(raw_data)),
  row.names = NULL
)

knitr::kable(variable_summary)
```

As we can see, the dataset contains student-level information, item-response variables, and the official INVALSI score. For the IRT model that we will use, we only need the binary item responses, so that's why we will delete all the other variables.  The official WLE score will however be kept separately for validation, because it is usefull to understand wheter we are predicting the student capacity properly or not.

# Select item-response variables

The item columns are the binary answers used in the IRT model.

The selected variables are the Italian test items from sections A, B, C, D, E and F. These variables form the response matrix used by the Bayesian IRT models.

`WLE_ITA_200` is kept separately because it is an external score, not an item response. It will be used later only to validate the Bayesian ability estimates.

```{r select-items}
item_cols <- c(
  paste0("A", 1:11),
  paste0("B", 1:11),
  "C1",
  paste0("D", 1:10),
  paste0("E", 1:6),
  paste0("F", 1:10)
)

missing_items <- setdiff(item_cols, names(raw_data))

if(length(missing_items) > 0) {
  stop("These item columns are missing: ", paste(missing_items, collapse = ", "))
}

Y_full <- raw_data %>%
  select(all_of(item_cols)) %>%
  as.data.frame()

Y_full <- as.matrix(Y_full)
storage.mode(Y_full) <- "numeric"

wle_full <- raw_data$WLE_ITA_200

cat("Dimension of the full item-response matrix Y_full:\n")
cat("Rows = students; columns = selected Italian test items.\n")
print(dim(Y_full))

cat("\nSummary statistics of the item responses:\n")
cat("These values refer to all 0/1 answers contained in the response matrix.\n")
cat("0 = incorrect answer; 1 = correct answer.\n")
print(summary(as.vector(Y_full)))

cat("\nSummary statistics of WLE_ITA_200:\n")
cat("This is the official INVALSI Italian ability score.\n")
cat("It is not used inside the Bayesian model, but only later for validation.\n")
print(summary(wle_full))
```

The output shows that the response matrix has 34,031 students and 49 selected items. The average item response is about 0.576, meaning that, across all students and all selected items, around 57.6% of answers are correct.

The summary of `WLE_ITA_200` describes the distribution of the official INVALSI Italian score. This score will later be compared with the Bayesian estimate of student ability.

# Check missing values

Before running the Bayesian IRT models, it is important to check whether the item-response matrix contains missing values.

Missing values are problematic because JAGS expects a clear response matrix for the Rasch and 2PL models. If some students have missing answers, the model becomes more complicated because we need to decide whether those values are truly missing, not administered, omitted, or missing by design.

In this dataset, there are no missing values in the selected item-response matrix and no missing values in `WLE_ITA_200`. Therefore, no rows are removed.

In our specific case, this section is kinda useless, because our data have already been cleaned by the the invalsi statistical offices, however I decided still to implement this chunk of code because, in case this code is going to be reused on other data, not as complete and clean as the one I used, it can be used for an easy initial data cleaning.

```{r check-missing}
cat("Number of missing values in the item-response matrix Y_full:\n")
print(sum(is.na(Y_full)))

cat("\nNumber of missing values in WLE_ITA_200:\n")
print(sum(is.na(wle_full)))

cat("\nNumber of incomplete rows:\n")
incomplete_rows <- !complete.cases(Y_full) | is.na(wle_full)
print(sum(incomplete_rows))

if(sum(incomplete_rows) > 0) {
  
  cat("\nSome incomplete rows were found.\n")
  cat("These rows will be removed before running the Bayesian models.\n")
  
  Y_full <- Y_full[!incomplete_rows, ]
  wle_full <- wle_full[!incomplete_rows]
  
} else {
  
  cat("\nNo incomplete rows were found.\n")
  cat("No rows are removed. The dataset is already complete for the selected variables.\n")
  
}

cat("\nFinal dimension of Y_full after the missing-value check:\n")
cat("Rows = students; columns = selected Italian test items.\n")
print(dim(Y_full))

cat("\nFinal summary of WLE_ITA_200 after the missing-value check:\n")
print(summary(wle_full))
```

After this check, the dataset is ready for sampling and for the Bayesian IRT models. Since no missing values are present, the full selected response matrix is kept unchanged.

# Sample students for MCMC

The full dataset is large, and a Bayesian IRT model estimates one ability parameter for each student. Therefore, using all students would make the MCMC algorithm impossible to handle on a normal laptop.

For this reason, I decided to use a random sample of students. This is a practical compromise: the model remains computationally feasible, but we still use real individual-level INVALSI data.

```{r sample-students}
# This function either creates a new random sample or reloads the previous one.
# In fast/final mode, it samples students and saves the sample in sampled_data.RData.
# In reuse mode, it reloads the exact same sample from sampled_data.RData.
load_or_create_sample <- function(Y_full,
                                  wle_full,
                                  n_sample,
                                  run_mode,
                                  results_dir = ".") {
  
  sample_file <- file.path(results_dir, "sampled_data.RData")
  
  if(run_mode == "reuse") {
    
    load(sample_file, envir = environment())
    
    required_sample_objects <- c("sample_id", "Y", "wle", "N", "J", "n_sample")
    missing_sample_objects <- required_sample_objects[!sapply(required_sample_objects, exists, envir = environment())]
    
    if(length(missing_sample_objects) > 0) {
      stop(
        "sampled_data.RData exists, but it is missing these objects: ",
        paste(missing_sample_objects, collapse = ", "),
        ". Re-run the project in fast or final mode with this updated file."
      )
    }
    
    cat("Reuse mode: sampled_data.RData loaded.\n")
    cat("The same sample used in the previous MCMC run is used again.\n\n")
    
  } else {
    
    if(n_sample > nrow(Y_full)) {
      n_sample <- nrow(Y_full)
    }
    
    sample_id <- sample(1:nrow(Y_full), n_sample)
    
    Y <- Y_full[sample_id, , drop = FALSE]
    wle <- wle_full[sample_id]
    
    N <- nrow(Y)
    J <- ncol(Y)
    n_sample <- N
    
    save(
      sample_id,
      Y,
      wle,
      N,
      J,
      n_sample,
      file = sample_file
    )
    
    cat("New sample created and saved in sampled_data.RData.\n\n")
  }
  
  list(
    sample_id = sample_id,
    Y = Y,
    wle = wle,
    N = N,
    J = J,
    n_sample = N
  )
}

sample_objects <- load_or_create_sample(
  Y_full = Y_full,
  wle_full = wle_full,
  n_sample = n_sample,
  run_mode = run_mode,
  results_dir = results_dir
)

list2env(sample_objects, envir = .GlobalEnv)

cat("Final response matrix used in the Bayesian models:\n")
cat("Rows = sampled students; columns = selected Italian test items.\n")
print(dim(Y))

cat("\nNumber of sampled students used in the model:\n")
print(N)

cat("\nNumber of selected test items used in the model:\n")
print(J)
```

The object `Y` is the final response matrix used in the Bayesian models.

`N` is the number of sampled students.  
`J` is the number of test items.

# Exploratory Data Analysis

Before estimating Bayesian models, it is better to take a look at raw data.

The aim of this section is to understand:

- how students performed on average;
- which items were easier or harder;
- whether the raw score is related to the official INVALSI score `WLE_ITA_200`.

```{r eda-objects}
# For the EDA, we use the full cleaned dataset.
# This gives a more general description of student performance and item difficulty.
Y_eda <- Y_full
wle_eda <- wle_full

# Student raw score: proportion of correct answers for each student
student_score <- rowMeans(Y_eda)

# Item score: proportion of students who answered each item correctly
item_score <- colMeans(Y_eda)

eda_students <- data.frame(
  student_score = student_score,
  WLE_ITA_200 = wle_eda
)

eda_items <- data.frame(
  item = colnames(Y_eda),
  proportion_correct = item_score
)

cat("Summary statistics of student raw scores:\n")
cat("Each value is the proportion of correct answers for one student in the full cleaned dataset.\n")
cat("For example, 0.60 means that the student answered 60% of the selected items correctly.\n")
print(summary(student_score))

cat("\nSummary statistics of item proportions correct:\n")
cat("Each value is the proportion of students in the full cleaned dataset who answered one item correctly.\n")
cat("A high value means that the item is easier; a low value means that the item is more difficult.\n")
print(summary(item_score))

cat("\nCorrelation between student raw score and WLE_ITA_200:\n")
cat("This checks whether students with more correct answers also tend to have a higher official INVALSI score.\n")
print(cor(student_score, wle_eda, use = "complete.obs"))
```

```{r eda-student-score}
ggplot(eda_students, aes(x = student_score)) +
  geom_histogram(bins = 100) +
  labs(
    title = "Distribution of student proportion correct",
    x = "Proportion of correct answers",
    y = "Number of students"
  )
```

This graph shows how student raw scores are distributed. As you can see, since the distribution is concentrated on the right, many students answered most items correctly. If it was concentrated on the left, many students answered only a small proportion of items correctly (but is not our case).

```{r eda-item-score}
ggplot(eda_items, aes(x = reorder(item, proportion_correct), y = proportion_correct)) +
  geom_col() +
  geom_text(
    aes(label = paste0(round(proportion_correct * 100, 1), "%")),
    hjust = -0.1,
    size = 3
  ) +
  coord_flip() +
  scale_y_continuous(
    limits = c(0, 1),
    labels = function(x) paste0(x * 100, "%")
  ) +
  labs(
    title = "Proportion of correct answers by item",
    subtitle = "Text labels show the observed probability of a correct answer for each item",
    x = "Item",
    y = "Observed probability of correct answer"
  )
```

This graph shows the observed easiness of each item. Items with high proportions are easier, while items with low proportions are more difficult.



```{r eda-wle-counts}
eda_students_count <- eda_students %>%
  dplyr::count(student_score, WLE_ITA_200)

ggplot(eda_students_count, aes(x = student_score, y = WLE_ITA_200, size = n)) +
  geom_point(alpha = 0.5) +
  geom_smooth(
    data = eda_students,
    aes(x = student_score, y = WLE_ITA_200),
    method = "loess",
    se = FALSE,
    inherit.aes = FALSE
  ) +
  labs(
    title = "Raw score vs official WLE score",
    subtitle = "Point size represents the number of students with the same raw score and WLE score",
    x = "Proportion correct",
    y = "WLE_ITA_200",
    size = "Number of students"
  )
```

This graph compares the simple raw score with the official INVALSI score `WLE_ITA_200`. A positive relationship is expected, because students with a higher proportion of correct answers should generally have a higher official WLE score.

Each point represents a combination of raw score and WLE score. Since many students can obtain the same number of correct answers, several students may overlap on the same point. For this reason, the size of the point represents the number of students with that same raw score and WLE score: larger points indicate that more students are concentrated there.

The graph shows a clear positive relationship between raw score and WLE score. However, the relationship is not perfectly linear, especially at the lower and upper extremes. Since the aim of this section is only exploratory, we do not investigate the exact mathematical form of this relationship in detail.



# Helper functions

This section defines helper functions used later in the project.

These functions are not part of the statistical model itself, but they are useful for organising the MCMC output and computing predictive measures.

```{r helper-functions}
sort_jags_cols <- function(cols) {
  index <- as.numeric(gsub(".*\\[|\\]", "", cols))
  cols[order(index)]
}

inv_logit <- function(x) {
  1 / (1 + exp(-x))
}

clip_probs <- function(p) {
  pmin(pmax(p, 1e-6), 1 - 1e-6)
}

rmse <- function(observed, predicted) {
  sqrt(mean((observed - predicted)^2, na.rm = TRUE))
}

mae <- function(observed, predicted) {
  mean(abs(observed - predicted), na.rm = TRUE)
}

jags_data <- list(
  Y = Y,
  N = N,
  J = J
)

cat("Data list prepared for JAGS:\n")
cat("The list contains the response matrix Y, the number of students N, and the number of items J.\n")
print(names(jags_data))

cat("\nNumber of students passed to JAGS:\n")
print(jags_data$N)

cat("\nNumber of items passed to JAGS:\n")
print(jags_data$J)
```

The function `sort_jags_cols()` is used to order parameters correctly. Without this function, R may order parameters as `theta[1]`, `theta[10]`, `theta[100]`, instead of `theta[1]`, `theta[2]`, `theta[3]`.

The function `inv_logit()` transforms linear predictions into probabilities between 0 and 1.

The functions `rmse()` and `mae()` are used later to compare observed and predicted scores.

# Model 1: Bayesian Rasch / 1PL model

The Rasch model is:

$$
Y_{ij} \sim Bernoulli(p_{ij})
$$

$$
logit(p_{ij}) = \theta_i - b_j
$$

where:

\[
\begin{aligned}
\theta_i &= \text{ability of student } i, \\
b_j &= \text{difficulty of item } j.
\end{aligned}
\]

The interpretation of the model is:

\[
\begin{aligned}
\theta_i > b_j &\Rightarrow p_{ij} > 0.5 
&&\text{the student is likely to answer correctly}, \\[6pt]
\theta_i < b_j &\Rightarrow p_{ij} < 0.5 
&&\text{the student is likely to answer incorrectly}, \\[6pt]
\theta_i = b_j &\Rightarrow p_{ij} = 0.5 
&&\text{the student has a fifty-fifty probability of answering correctly}.
\end{aligned}
\]

The Rasch model is the simplest IRT model used in this project. It estimates one latent ability parameter for each student and one difficulty parameter for each item. The model assumes that all items have the same discrimination power. Therefore, items can differ in difficulty, but they cannot differ in how strongly they separate weaker and stronger students.That will be the feature that the follow and more complex 2pl model will introduce

```{r rasch-model}
rasch_model <- "
model {

  for(i in 1:N) {

    theta[i] ~ dnorm(0, 1)

    for(j in 1:J) {

      Y[i, j] ~ dbern(p[i, j])

      logit(p[i, j]) <- theta[i] - b[j]

      log_lik[i, j] <- Y[i, j] * log(p[i, j]) +
                       (1 - Y[i, j]) * log(1 - p[i, j])

      dev_contribution[i, j] <- -2 * log_lik[i, j]

    }

    student_deviance[i] <- sum(dev_contribution[i, 1:J])
  }

  for(j in 1:J) {

    b[j] ~ dnorm(0, 1)

  }

  total_deviance <- sum(student_deviance[1:N])
}
"

writeLines(rasch_model, "rasch_model.txt")
```

# Run the Rasch model

In this section, we estimate the Rasch / 1PL model using MCMC. The goal is to obtain posterior samples for the latent student abilities `theta` and the item difficulties `b`.

In the Bayesian framework, the posterior distribution is proportional to the likelihood times the prior. The likelihood tells us how well a given combination of `theta` and `b` explains the observed response matrix, while the priors define our initial assumptions about the parameters.

Since the posterior distribution of the Rasch model is too complex to compute analytically, we use MCMC through JAGS. MCMC generates a sequence of dependent samples. Values of `theta` and `b` that have higher posterior probability are visited more often by the chain. In this way, after convergence, the collected samples approximate the posterior distribution of the model parameters.

The posterior samples can then be used to compute posterior means, credible intervals and convergence diagnostics for both student abilities and item difficulties.

```{r rasch-inits}
if(!reuse_existing_results) {
  inits_rasch <- vector("list", n_chains)
  
  for(chain in 1:n_chains) {
    inits_rasch[[chain]] <- list(
      theta = rnorm(N, 0, 0.2),
      b = rnorm(J, 0, 0.2)
    )
  }
}
```

```{r run-rasch, cache=TRUE}
if(reuse_existing_results) {

  load(file.path(results_dir, "rasch_results.RData"), envir = .GlobalEnv)
  cat("Reuse mode: Rasch / 1PL posterior samples loaded from rasch_results.RData.\n")

} else {

  if(run_dic) {
    load.module("dic")
  }

  jags_rasch <- jags.model(
    file = "rasch_model.txt",
    data = jags_data,
    inits = inits_rasch,
    n.chains = n_chains,
    n.adapt = n_adapt
  )

  update(jags_rasch, burn_in)

  samples_rasch <- coda.samples(
    model = jags_rasch,
    variable.names = c("theta", "b"),
    n.iter = n_iter,
    thin = thin_value
  )

  if(run_dic) {
    deviance_trace_rasch <- coda.samples(
      model = jags_rasch,
      variable.names = c("total_deviance"),
      n.iter = n_iter,
      thin = thin_value
    )

    dic_rasch <- dic.samples(
      model = jags_rasch,
      n.iter = n_iter,
      thin = thin_value,
      type = "pD"
    )
  }
}
```

# Rasch MCMC diagnostics

Before interpreting the posterior estimates, it is important to check whether the MCMC algorithm worked properly. In Bayesian analysis, we do not observe the posterior distribution directly. Instead, we approximate it using MCMC simulation. Therefore, convergence diagnostics are necessary to check whether the chains are stable and whether the saved draws are reliable.
```{r rasch-diagnostics}
cat("Posterior summary of the Rasch model parameters:\n")
cat("This table reports posterior means, standard deviations, and credible intervals for theta and b.\n")
cat("theta parameters represent student abilities.\n")
cat("b parameters represent item difficulties.\n\n")
print(summary(samples_rasch))

diagnostic_params_rasch <- c(
  "b[1]", "b[2]", "b[3]", "b[4]", "b[5]", "b[6]",
  "theta[1]", "theta[2]", "theta[3]", "theta[4]", "theta[5]", "theta[6]"
)

cat("\nSelected parameters used for convergence diagnostics:\n")
cat("We do not inspect all theta and b parameters because there are many of them.\n")
cat("Instead, we inspect a small set of item difficulties and student abilities.\n\n")
print(diagnostic_params_rasch)

gelman_rasch <- gelman.diag(samples_rasch[, diagnostic_params_rasch])
ess_rasch <- effectiveSize(samples_rasch[, diagnostic_params_rasch])

cat("\nGelman-Rubin convergence diagnostic for selected Rasch parameters:\n")
cat("The Gelman-Rubin diagnostic compares the MCMC chains.\n")
cat("Values close to 1 indicate that the chains have converged to the same posterior distribution.\n")
cat("As a practical rule, values below 1.05 are very good, and values below 1.10 are usually acceptable.\n\n")
print(gelman_rasch)

max_gelman_upper <- max(gelman_rasch$psrf[, "Upper C.I."])

cat("\nInterpretation of the Gelman-Rubin diagnostic for selected parameters:\n")

if(max_gelman_upper < 1.05) {
  cat("The largest upper confidence interval among selected parameters is", round(max_gelman_upper, 3), ".\n")
  cat("This is very close to 1, so the selected Rasch parameters show very good convergence.\n")
} else if(max_gelman_upper < 1.10) {
  cat("The largest upper confidence interval among selected parameters is", round(max_gelman_upper, 3), ".\n")
  cat("This is acceptable, although convergence should still be checked with traceplots.\n")
} else {
  cat("The largest upper confidence interval among selected parameters is", round(max_gelman_upper, 3), ".\n")
  cat("This may indicate convergence problems. More iterations or better starting values may be needed.\n")
}

cat("\nEffective sample size for selected Rasch parameters:\n")
cat("The effective sample size measures how much independent information is contained in the MCMC draws.\n")
cat("Because MCMC draws are autocorrelated, the effective sample size is usually lower than the total number of saved draws.\n")
cat("Higher values are better.\n\n")
print(round(ess_rasch, 2))

saved_draws_per_chain <- niter(samples_rasch)
number_of_chains <- nchain(samples_rasch)
total_saved_draws <- saved_draws_per_chain * number_of_chains

cat("\nNumber of saved posterior draws:\n")
cat("Saved draws per chain:", saved_draws_per_chain, "\n")
cat("Number of chains:", number_of_chains, "\n")
cat("Total saved draws:", total_saved_draws, "\n")

cat("\nInterpretation of the effective sample size for selected parameters:\n")
cat("The maximum possible effective sample size is approximately equal to the total number of saved draws.\n")
cat("Here, the total number of saved draws is", total_saved_draws, ".\n")
cat("If an effective sample size is close to this value, the MCMC draws contain almost as much information as independent draws.\n")
cat("If an effective sample size is much lower, the chain has more autocorrelation for that parameter.\n\n")

min_ess <- min(ess_rasch)
max_ess <- max(ess_rasch)

cat("Minimum effective sample size among selected parameters:", round(min_ess, 2), "\n")
cat("Maximum effective sample size among selected parameters:", round(max_ess, 2), "\n")

if(min_ess > 400) {
  cat("All selected parameters have a good effective sample size for this run.\n")
} else if(min_ess > 200) {
  cat("The effective sample sizes are acceptable.\n")
} else {
  cat("Some effective sample sizes are low. More MCMC iterations may be useful.\n")
}

# General diagnostics for all Rasch parameters

all_params_rasch <- varnames(samples_rasch)

all_params_rasch <- all_params_rasch[
  grepl("^b\\[", all_params_rasch) |
  grepl("^theta\\[", all_params_rasch)
]

cat("\nGeneral diagnostics for all Rasch parameters:\n")
cat("The following diagnostics are computed using all monitored item difficulties and student abilities.\n")
cat("This gives a broader picture than the selected subset used above.\n\n")

gelman_all_rasch <- gelman.diag(
  samples_rasch[, all_params_rasch],
  multivariate = FALSE
)

gelman_all_psrf <- gelman_all_rasch$psrf

max_gelman_upper_all <- max(gelman_all_psrf[, "Upper C.I."], na.rm = TRUE)
max_gelman_upper_all_param <- rownames(gelman_all_psrf)[
  which.max(gelman_all_psrf[, "Upper C.I."])
]

cat("Highest Gelman-Rubin upper confidence interval among all Rasch parameters:\n")
cat(max_gelman_upper_all_param, "=", round(max_gelman_upper_all, 3), "\n")

if(max_gelman_upper_all < 1.05) {
  cat("Overall, the Rasch model shows very good convergence across all monitored parameters.\n")
} else if(max_gelman_upper_all < 1.10) {
  cat("Overall, convergence is acceptable, although some parameters should still be checked carefully.\n")
} else {
  cat("At least one parameter may have convergence problems. More iterations or better starting values may be useful.\n")
}

ess_all_rasch <- effectiveSize(samples_rasch[, all_params_rasch])

min_ess_all <- min(ess_all_rasch, na.rm = TRUE)
max_ess_all <- max(ess_all_rasch, na.rm = TRUE)

min_ess_all_param <- names(ess_all_rasch)[which.min(ess_all_rasch)]
max_ess_all_param <- names(ess_all_rasch)[which.max(ess_all_rasch)]

cat("\nEffective sample size among all Rasch parameters:\n")
cat("Lowest effective sample size:", min_ess_all_param, "=", round(min_ess_all, 2), "\n")
cat("Highest effective sample size:", max_ess_all_param, "=", round(max_ess_all, 2), "\n")

if(min_ess_all > 400) {
  cat("All Rasch parameters have a good effective sample size for this run.\n")
} else if(min_ess_all > 200) {
  cat("The effective sample sizes are generally acceptable, although some parameters are less efficiently sampled.\n")
} else {
  cat("Some Rasch parameters have low effective sample size. More MCMC iterations may be useful.\n")
}
```

```{r rasch-trace-b}
# Traceplots are used to check the behaviour of the MCMC chains over iterations.
# Each line shows how the sampled values of a parameter move during the simulation.
# For the Rasch model, b[j] represents the difficulty of item j.
# A good traceplot should look like a stable random cloud, without clear trends.
# If the chains mix well and move around the same area, this suggests good convergence.
# If the chains are separated, stuck, or show trends, the MCMC may not have converged well.

traceplot(samples_rasch[, c("b[1]", "b[2]", "b[3]", "b[4]", "b[5]", "b[6]")])
```

The traceplots above show the MCMC chains for the first six item difficulty parameters. Each curve represents the sampled posterior values of one parameter across iterations. A good traceplot should fluctuate around a stable region without a visible upward or downward trend, and this is basically what we are seeing here. If the chains overlap and mix well, this suggests that the MCMC algorithm is exploring the posterior distribution properly. If the chains remain separated or show long trends, convergence may be problematic.

```{r rasch-trace-theta}
# These traceplots show the MCMC chains for the first six student ability parameters.
# In the Rasch model, theta[i] represents the latent ability of student i.
# The purpose is to check whether the posterior samples are stable across iterations.
# Good traceplots should fluctuate around a constant level, with no increasing or decreasing trend.
# If the chains overlap and mix well, this indicates that the MCMC is exploring the posterior distribution properly.
# If the plot shows poor mixing, long trends, or chains moving in different regions, the estimates may be unreliable.

traceplot(samples_rasch[, c("theta[1]", "theta[2]", "theta[3]", "theta[4]", "theta[5]", "theta[6]")])
```

The traceplots above show the MCMC chains for the first six student ability parameters. These plots are useful because the Rasch model estimates one latent ability value for each student. Stable and well-mixed chains indicate that the posterior estimates of student ability are reliable for the selected parameters. Since there are many ability parameters, i have shown here only a small subset is shown for visual inspection (the ability of only the first 6 students).

# Rasch posterior estimates

This section extracts the posterior means of student abilities and item difficulties.
Section 13 checked whether the MCMC samples were reliable.
Here, we transform those samples into clean student-level and item-level tables, which will be used in the following interpretation, plots, rankings, and comparisons.

```{r rasch-posterior-means}
rasch_matrix <- as.matrix(samples_rasch)

theta_cols <- sort_jags_cols(grep("^theta\\[", colnames(rasch_matrix), value = TRUE))
b_cols <- sort_jags_cols(grep("^b\\[", colnames(rasch_matrix), value = TRUE))

theta_hat_1pl <- colMeans(rasch_matrix[, theta_cols])
b_hat_1pl <- colMeans(rasch_matrix[, b_cols])

# Raw scores and item proportions must be computed on the same sampled data used by the model
student_score_1pl <- rowMeans(Y)
item_score_1pl <- colMeans(Y)


results_students_1pl <- data.frame(
  student_id = sample_id,
  theta_hat_1pl = theta_hat_1pl,
  raw_score = student_score_1pl,
  WLE_ITA_200 = wle
)

results_items_1pl <- data.frame(
  item = colnames(Y),
  b_hat_1pl = b_hat_1pl,
  proportion_correct = item_score_1pl
)

cat("First rows of the student-level Rasch results:\n")
cat("Each row is one sampled student, with estimated ability, raw score, and WLE score.\n")
print(head(results_students_1pl))

cat("\nFirst rows of the item-level Rasch results:\n")
cat("Each row is one item, with estimated difficulty and observed proportion correct.\n")
print(head(results_items_1pl))
```

# Interpret Rasch item difficulty

In the Rasch model:

- high `b_hat_1pl` = difficult item;
- low `b_hat_1pl` = easy item.

The observed proportion correct has the opposite interpretation:

- high proportion correct = easy item;
- low proportion correct = difficult item.

Therefore, we expect a negative correlation between estimated difficulty and observed proportion correct.

```{r rasch-item-results}
# We add two ranking columns to compare item difficulty in two different ways:
# 1. Ranking based on Bayesian Rasch difficulty b_hat_1pl.
# 2. Ranking based on the observed proportion of correct answers.
# For b_hat_1pl, lower values mean easier items.
# For proportion_correct, higher values mean easier items.

results_items_1pl <- results_items_1pl %>%
  mutate(
    rank_b_hat_easy_to_hard = rank(b_hat_1pl, ties.method = "first"),
    rank_prop_easy_to_hard = rank(-proportion_correct, ties.method = "first"),
    rank_difference = rank_b_hat_easy_to_hard - rank_prop_easy_to_hard,
    abs_rank_difference = abs(rank_difference)
  ) %>%
  arrange(rank_b_hat_easy_to_hard)

cor_b1_itemscore <- cor(
  results_items_1pl$b_hat_1pl,
  results_items_1pl$proportion_correct
)

cor_rank_b_prop <- cor(
  results_items_1pl$rank_b_hat_easy_to_hard,
  results_items_1pl$rank_prop_easy_to_hard,
  method = "spearman"
)

cat("Correlation between Rasch difficulty and observed item proportion correct:\n")
cat("This value should usually be negative: difficult items should have lower proportions correct.\n")
print(cor_b1_itemscore)

cat("\nSpearman correlation between the two item difficulty rankings:\n")
cat("This value should usually be positive and high if the two rankings are similar.\n")
print(cor_rank_b_prop)

cat("\nItem-level Rasch results ordered from easiest to hardest according to b_hat_1pl:\n")
print(
  results_items_1pl %>%
    select(
      item,
      b_hat_1pl,
      proportion_correct,
      rank_b_hat_easy_to_hard,
      rank_prop_easy_to_hard,
      rank_difference,
      abs_rank_difference
    )
)
```

The table compares the model-based difficulty ranking with the raw observed difficulty ranking. The column `rank_b_hat_easy_to_hard` ranks items using the posterior mean Rasch difficulty: rank 1 is the easiest item. The column `rank_prop_easy_to_hard` ranks items using the observed proportion correct: the item with the highest proportion correct receives rank 1 because it was the easiest in the raw data. If the Rasch model is coherent with the observed answers, the two rankings should be very similar. The column `abs_rank_difference` helps identify items where the two approaches disagree more strongly.  As you can see, the two approaches never disagree, and this shows actually how much b_hat is close to the difficulty of the question and explain why the spearman index is 1.

```{r rasch-item-difficulty-plot}
eps <- 1e-6

results_items_1pl <- results_items_1pl %>%
  mutate(
    proportion_correct_safe = pmin(pmax(proportion_correct, eps), 1 - eps),
    observed_difficulty_from_prop = -qlogis(proportion_correct_safe),
    difficulty_difference = b_hat_1pl - observed_difficulty_from_prop
  ) %>%
  arrange(b_hat_1pl) %>%
  mutate(item_ordered = factor(item, levels = item))

plot_items_1pl <- results_items_1pl %>%
  select(item_ordered, b_hat_1pl, observed_difficulty_from_prop) %>%
  pivot_longer(
    cols = c(b_hat_1pl, observed_difficulty_from_prop),
    names_to = "measure",
    values_to = "difficulty_value"
  ) %>%
  mutate(
    measure = recode(
      measure,
      b_hat_1pl = "Bayesian Rasch difficulty",
      observed_difficulty_from_prop = "Observed difficulty from proportion correct"
    )
  )

difference_labels_1pl <- results_items_1pl %>%
  mutate(
    measure = "Bayesian Rasch difficulty",
    difference_label = round(difficulty_difference, 2)
  )

ggplot(plot_items_1pl, aes(x = item_ordered, y = difficulty_value, fill = measure)) +
  geom_col(position = position_dodge(width = 0.8), width = 0.7) +
  geom_text(
    data = difference_labels_1pl,
    aes(
      x = item_ordered,
      y = b_hat_1pl,
      label = difference_label,
      group = measure
    ),
    position = position_dodge(width = 0.8),
    hjust = ifelse(difference_labels_1pl$b_hat_1pl >= 0, -0.1, 1.1),
    size = 2.8,
    inherit.aes = FALSE
  ) +
  coord_flip() +
  scale_y_continuous(expand = expansion(mult = c(0.15, 0.25))) +
  labs(
    title = "Bayesian Rasch difficulty compared with observed item difficulty",
    subtitle = "Text labels show the numerical difference: Bayesian Rasch difficulty minus observed difficulty",
    x = "Item",
    y = "Difficulty value",
    fill = "Measure"
  ) +
  theme(
    legend.position = "bottom"
  )
```

This graph compares the posterior mean difficulty estimated by the Bayesian Rasch model with an observed difficulty index derived from the proportion of correct answers. Since `proportion_correct` and `b_hat_1pl` are not originally on the same scale, the observed proportion was transformed using `-logit(proportion_correct)`. After this transformation, both measures have the same interpretation: lower values indicate easier items, while higher values indicate more difficult items.

The graph also reports, at the end of each Bayesian Rasch bar, the difference between the two measures. This difference is calculated as `Bayesian Rasch difficulty - observed difficulty`. Therefore, a positive difference means that the Rasch model estimates the item as more difficult than what is suggested by the observed proportion correct. A negative difference means that the Rasch model estimates the item as easier than the observed difficulty index.

Overall, the Bayesian Rasch estimates and the observed difficulty values follow a similar pattern, meaning that the model and the raw data generally agree on which items are easier and which items are harder. However, the differences shown on the graph make clear that the model does not simply reproduce the observed proportions. For some items, the difference is small, so the model-based difficulty is very close to the raw-data difficulty. For other items, the difference is larger, meaning that the Rasch model adjusts the difficulty estimate more substantially.

This happens because the Rasch model estimates item difficulty using the whole response pattern of the students, not only the percentage of correct answers for each single item. As a result, the model-based difficulty can differ from the simple observed difficulty, especially for items where the response pattern contains additional information that is not captured by the raw proportion correct alone.

```{r rasch-item-difficulty-scatter, fig.width=10, fig.height=7}
ggplot(results_items_1pl, aes(x = proportion_correct, y = b_hat_1pl, label = item)) +
  geom_point(size = 2) +
  geom_smooth(method = "lm", se = FALSE) +
  geom_label_repel(
    size = 3,
    max.overlaps = Inf,
    box.padding = 0.35,
    point.padding = 0.25,
    min.segment.length = 0,
    segment.size = 0.3
  ) +
  labs(
    title = "Observed item performance vs Rasch difficulty",
    x = "Observed proportion correct",
    y = "Posterior mean difficulty"
  ) +
  theme(
    plot.margin = margin(10, 20, 10, 10)
  )
```

This scatter plot compares the observed proportion correct with the estimated Rasch difficulty for each item. A negative relationship is expected: items answered correctly by many students should have low estimated difficulty, while items answered correctly by few students should have high estimated difficulty. Therefore, this plot is a simple visual check that the Bayesian difficulty estimates are coherent with the raw item performance.

# Rasch item characteristic curves

Item characteristic curves show the relationship between student ability and the probability of answering an item correctly. In the Rasch model, all items have the same discrimination, so the curves have the same general steepness. What changes from item to item is mainly the horizontal position of the curve, which depends on item difficulty.

```{r rasch-icc-selected-items}
# Select representative Rasch items.
selected_items_1pl <- bind_rows(
  results_items_1pl %>%
    slice_min(b_hat_1pl, n = 1, with_ties = FALSE) %>%
    mutate(type = "Easiest item"),
  results_items_1pl %>%
    slice_max(b_hat_1pl, n = 1, with_ties = FALSE) %>%
    mutate(type = "Hardest item"),
  results_items_1pl %>%
    mutate(abs_difficulty = abs(b_hat_1pl)) %>%
    slice_min(abs_difficulty, n = 1, with_ties = FALSE) %>%
    select(-abs_difficulty) %>%
    mutate(type = "Average-difficulty item"),
  results_items_1pl %>%
    mutate(abs_from_half = abs(proportion_correct - 0.5)) %>%
    slice_min(abs_from_half, n = 1, with_ties = FALSE) %>%
    select(-abs_from_half) %>%
    mutate(type = "Most balanced observed item")
) %>%
  distinct(item, .keep_all = TRUE) %>%
  mutate(
    item_label = paste0(item, " - ", type),
    difficulty_label = paste0("b = ", round(b_hat_1pl, 2))
  )

theta_grid_1pl <- seq(-4, 4, length.out = 200)

icc_data_1pl <- selected_items_1pl %>%
  tidyr::crossing(theta = theta_grid_1pl) %>%
  mutate(
    probability_correct = plogis(theta - b_hat_1pl)
  )

ggplot(icc_data_1pl, aes(x = theta, y = probability_correct, group = item_label)) +
  geom_line(linewidth = 1) +
  geom_hline(yintercept = 0.5, linetype = "dashed") +
  geom_vline(
    data = selected_items_1pl,
    aes(xintercept = b_hat_1pl),
    linetype = "dashed",
    inherit.aes = FALSE
  ) +
  geom_text(
    data = selected_items_1pl,
    aes(x = b_hat_1pl, y = 0.55, label = difficulty_label),
    inherit.aes = FALSE,
    angle = 90,
    vjust = -0.5,
    size = 3
  ) +
  labs(
    title = "Item characteristic curves for selected Rasch items",
    subtitle = "The dashed lines show the item difficulty, where the probability of a correct answer is 50%",
    x = "Student ability",
    y = "Probability of correct answer"
  ) +
  facet_wrap(~ item_label)
```

**Interpretation of the Rasch item characteristic curves.**  
These curves show how the probability of answering an item correctly changes as student ability increases. The x-axis represents student ability, while the y-axis represents the predicted probability of a correct answer. In the Rasch model, the probability depends on the difference between student ability and item difficulty. When student ability is equal to item difficulty, the predicted probability of a correct answer is 0.5.

Curves shifted to the left represent easier items, because even students with lower ability have a relatively high probability of answering correctly. Curves shifted to the right represent more difficult items, because students need a higher ability level to reach the same probability of success. Therefore, item difficulty is mainly represented by the horizontal position of the curve.

The steepness of the curve represents how strongly an item separates students with lower and higher ability. However, in the Rasch model, all items are assumed to have the same discrimination level. For this reason, the curves have the same basic steepness and differ mainly in their horizontal position. This is why the graph is especially useful for visualizing item difficulty rather than differences in item discrimination.

# Rasch item information curves

Item information curves show where an item is most useful for estimating student ability. In the Rasch model, information is highest around the difficulty level of the item. This means that an item is most informative for students whose ability is close to that item's difficulty.

```{r rasch-information-selected-items}
# For the Rasch model, item discrimination is fixed to 1.
# Therefore, item information is p * (1 - p).
# Information is highest when the predicted probability is close to 0.5.
# This happens when student ability is equal to item difficulty.

information_data_1pl <- selected_items_1pl %>%
  tidyr::crossing(theta = theta_grid_1pl) %>%
  mutate(
    probability_correct = plogis(theta - b_hat_1pl),
    item_information = probability_correct * (1 - probability_correct),
    item_label = paste0(item, " - ", type)
  )

difficulty_labels_1pl <- selected_items_1pl %>%
  mutate(
    item_label = paste0(item, " - ", type),
    difficulty_label = paste0("b = ", round(b_hat_1pl, 2))
  )

ggplot(information_data_1pl, aes(x = theta, y = item_information, group = item_label)) +
  geom_line(linewidth = 1) +
  geom_vline(
    data = difficulty_labels_1pl,
    aes(xintercept = b_hat_1pl),
    linetype = "dashed",
    inherit.aes = FALSE
  ) +
  geom_text(
    data = difficulty_labels_1pl,
    aes(
      x = b_hat_1pl,
      y = max(information_data_1pl$item_information),
      label = difficulty_label
    ),
    angle = 90,
    vjust = -0.4,
    hjust = 1.1,
    size = 3,
    inherit.aes = FALSE
  ) +
  labs(
    title = "Item information curves for selected Rasch items",
    subtitle = "Dashed lines show the estimated difficulty of each item",
    x = "Student ability",
    y = "Item information"
  ) +
  facet_wrap(~ item_label)
```

**Interpretation of the Rasch item information curves.**  
These curves show at which ability levels each item is most useful for estimating student ability. In the Rasch model, item information is calculated as `p * (1 - p)`, where `p` is the predicted probability of answering correctly. Information is highest when this probability is close to 0.5, because this is the point where the item is neither too easy nor too difficult for the student. At this point, the response is most informative for understanding whether the student's ability is slightly above or below the item's difficulty.

The dashed vertical line shows the estimated difficulty of each item. The peak of each curve is located around this difficulty value. This means that each item is most informative for students whose ability is close to the item difficulty. Easier items, such as A3, provide more information for lower-ability students. Harder items, such as E6, provide more information for higher-ability students. Average-difficulty items, such as F6, provide more information around the middle of the ability scale.

Since this is a Rasch model, all items are assumed to have the same discrimination. Therefore, the information curves have a similar shape and approximately the same maximum height. For this reason, in the 1PL/Rasch model, the most important aspect is not how high the peak is, but where the peak is located on the ability scale. The graph mainly tells us which type of student each item is most informative for.

This would be different in a 2PL model, where each item has its own discrimination parameter. In that case, the height of the information curve would also matter, because more discriminating items would have higher peaks and would therefore provide more information. In other words, in the Rasch model items mainly differ by the location of their information peak, while in the 2PL model items can also differ by how much maximum information they provide.


# Validate Rasch ability with WLE

The model does not use `WLE_ITA_200` during estimation. Therefore, WLE can be used as an external validation score.

If the estimated ability is meaningful, `theta_hat_1pl` should be positively correlated with `WLE_ITA_200`.

```{r rasch-wle-validation}
cor_theta1_wle <- cor(
  results_students_1pl$theta_hat_1pl,
  results_students_1pl$WLE_ITA_200,
  use = "complete.obs"
)

cor_theta1_raw <- cor(
  results_students_1pl$theta_hat_1pl,
  results_students_1pl$raw_score,
  use = "complete.obs"
)

cat("Correlation between Rasch ability estimate and WLE_ITA_200:\n")
cat("A positive value means that the Bayesian ability estimate is coherent with the official INVALSI score.\n")
print(cor_theta1_wle)

cat("\nCorrelation between Rasch ability estimate and raw proportion correct:\n")
cat("A high positive value is expected because both variables measure student performance.\n")
print(cor_theta1_raw)
```

```{r rasch-theta-wle-plot}
ggplot(results_students_1pl, aes(x = theta_hat_1pl, y = WLE_ITA_200)) +
  geom_point(alpha = 0.4) +
  geom_smooth(method = "lm", se = FALSE) +
  labs(
    title = "Rasch ability estimate vs official WLE score",
    x = "Posterior mean theta from Rasch model",
    y = "WLE_ITA_200"
  )
```

This graph compares the Bayesian Rasch ability estimate with the official INVALSI `WLE_ITA_200` score. The model does not use `WLE_ITA_200` during estimation, so this comparison works as an external validation check. A positive and approximately linear relationship indicates that the Bayesian ability estimates are coherent with the official INVALSI proficiency score. The two variables are not expected to be identical because they are estimated using different methods and are expressed on different scales.

```{r rasch-theta-raw-plot}
ggplot(results_students_1pl, aes(x = theta_hat_1pl, y = raw_score)) +
  geom_point(alpha = 0.4) +
  geom_smooth(method = "lm", se = FALSE) +
  labs(
    title = "Rasch ability estimate vs raw proportion correct",
    x = "Posterior mean theta from Rasch model",
    y = "Raw proportion correct"
  )
```

This graph compares the posterior mean ability estimated by the Rasch model with the raw proportion of correct answers obtained by each student. The relationship is strongly positive, as expected: students with higher estimated ability also tend to have a higher proportion of correct answers. This confirms that the Rasch ability parameter is coherent with the observed performance in the test. However, the relationship is not perfectly identical because the Rasch model does not only count the number of correct answers, but estimates ability by considering the difficulty structure of the items.

```{r rasch-theta-distribution-binned}
# This plot shows the distribution of the estimated student abilities.
# We divide theta_hat_1pl into intervals of width 0.18.
# The height of each bar tells us how many students fall inside that ability interval.

theta_breaks <- seq(-4, 4, by = 0.18)

results_students_1pl_binned <- results_students_1pl %>%
  mutate(
    theta_bin = cut(
      theta_hat_1pl,
      breaks = theta_breaks,
      include.lowest = TRUE,
      right = FALSE
    )
  ) %>%
  filter(!is.na(theta_bin))

ggplot(results_students_1pl_binned, aes(x = theta_bin)) +
  geom_bar() +
  geom_text(
    stat = "count",
    aes(
      label = after_stat(
        paste0(round(count / sum(count) * 100, 1), "%")
      )
    ),
    vjust = -0.3,
    size = 3
  ) +
  labs(
    title = "Distribution of students by estimated Rasch ability",
    x = "Posterior mean theta interval",
    y = "Number of students"
  ) +
  scale_y_continuous(
    expand = expansion(mult = c(0, 0.10))
  ) +
  theme(
    axis.text.x = element_text(angle = 90, vjust = 0.5, hjust = 1)
  )
```

This plot shows the distribution of the posterior mean Rasch ability estimates across students. Each bar represents an interval of theta values, and the height of each bar indicates how many students fall within that ability range. This graph is useful to understand whether most students are concentrated around the average ability level and whether there are many very low-ability or very high-ability students.

# Rasch posterior predictive check

Posterior predictive checking means:

1. estimate the model;
2. simulate fake answers from the fitted model;
3. compare fake data with real data.

If the model fits well, the fake data should look similar to the observed data.

```{r rasch-posterior-predictive}
p_hat_1pl <- matrix(NA, nrow = N, ncol = J)

for(j in 1:J) {
  p_hat_1pl[, j] <- inv_logit(theta_hat_1pl - b_hat_1pl[j])
}

Y_rep_1pl <- matrix(
  rbinom(N * J, size = 1, prob = as.vector(p_hat_1pl)),
  nrow = N,
  ncol = J
)

observed_student_score <- rowMeans(Y)
expected_student_score_1pl <- rowMeans(p_hat_1pl)
replicated_student_score_1pl <- rowMeans(Y_rep_1pl)

observed_item_score <- colMeans(Y)
expected_item_score_1pl <- colMeans(p_hat_1pl)
replicated_item_score_1pl <- colMeans(Y_rep_1pl)

ppc_students_1pl <- data.frame(
  observed = observed_student_score,
  expected_prediction = expected_student_score_1pl,
  replicated = replicated_student_score_1pl
)

ppc_items_1pl <- data.frame(
  item = colnames(Y),
  observed = observed_item_score,
  expected_prediction = expected_item_score_1pl,
  replicated = replicated_item_score_1pl
)
```

```{r rasch-ppc-students}
ggplot(ppc_students_1pl, aes(x = observed, y = expected_prediction)) +
  geom_point(alpha = 0.4) +
  geom_abline(intercept = 0, slope = 1, linetype = "dashed") +
  labs(
    title = "Posterior predictive check: student scores, Rasch model",
    x = "Observed proportion correct",
    y = "Expected predicted proportion correct"
  )
```

Each point represents one student. The x-axis shows the observed proportion of correct answers obtained by the student, while the y-axis shows the expected proportion of correct answers predicted by the Rasch model.

The dashed diagonal line represents perfect agreement between observed and predicted performance. If a point lies exactly on this line, it means that the model prediction is equal to the student’s observed score. Points close to the line indicate that the model is able to reproduce student performance quite well.

Overall, most points are close to the diagonal line, so the Rasch model seems to capture the general pattern of student ability reasonably well. Students with low observed scores also receive low predicted scores, while students with high observed scores receive high predicted scores. This means that the estimated ability parameter `theta` is consistent with the actual number of correct answers.

However, the graph also shows a small systematic pattern. For students with very low observed scores, many points are above the diagonal line. This means that the model slightly overpredicts their performance: it expects them to answer correctly a bit more often than they actually did. On the other hand, for students with very high observed scores, several points are below the diagonal line. This means that the model slightly underpredicts their performance: it expects a slightly lower proportion of correct answers than the one actually observed.

This behaviour is quite common in Rasch models, because predictions are pulled toward the general structure of the model. The model does not simply reproduce the raw score, but estimates student ability by combining student performance with item difficulties. Therefore, extreme observed performances can be slightly smoothed: very weak students may be predicted a little higher, and very strong students may be predicted a little lower.

In conclusion, the posterior predictive check suggests that the Rasch model fits the student scores reasonably well, because the points follow the diagonal line closely. At the same time, the small deviations at the extremes suggest that the model may slightly compress the differences between very low-performing and very high-performing students.

```{r rasch-ppc-items, fig.width=10, fig.height=7}
ggplot(ppc_items_1pl, aes(x = observed, y = expected_prediction, label = item)) +
  geom_point(size = 2) +
  geom_abline(intercept = 0, slope = 1, linetype = "dashed") +
  geom_label_repel(
    size = 3,
    max.overlaps = Inf,
    box.padding = 0.35,
    point.padding = 0.25,
    min.segment.length = 0,
    segment.size = 0.3
  ) +
  labs(
    title = "Posterior predictive check: item scores, Rasch model",
    x = "Observed item proportion correct",
    y = "Expected predicted item proportion correct"
  ) +
  coord_cartesian(clip = "off") +
  theme(
    plot.margin = margin(10, 30, 10, 10)
  )
```

Each point represents one item. The x-axis shows the observed proportion of students who answered that item correctly, while the y-axis shows the average predicted probability of a correct answer for that item. The dashed diagonal line represents perfect agreement between observed and predicted item performance. If the points are close to the line, the Rasch model reproduces the observed item difficulty structure well.
As you can see, basically the prediction on item difficulty is almost perfect

```{r rasch-ppc-hist}
df_ppc_students_1pl <- data.frame(
  score = c(observed_student_score, replicated_student_score_1pl),
  data_type = rep(c("Observed data", "Replicated data from Rasch"), each = N)
)

ggplot(df_ppc_students_1pl, aes(x = score, fill = data_type)) +
  geom_histogram(alpha = 0.5, position = "identity", bins = 98) +
  labs(
    title = "Observed vs replicated student scores, Rasch model",
    x = "Proportion correct",
    y = "Number of students",
    fill = "Data type"
  ) +
  theme(
    legend.position = "bottom",
    legend.title = element_text(size = 10),
    legend.text = element_text(size = 9)
  )
```

This histogram compares the distribution of the real observed student scores with the distribution of replicated scores simulated from the fitted Rasch model. The observed scores are the actual proportions of correct answers obtained by the students. The replicated scores are artificial scores generated by the model after estimating student abilities and item difficulties.

The purpose of this posterior predictive check is to understand whether the Rasch model is able to generate data similar to the data we actually observed. If the two histograms overlap strongly, this means that the model reproduces the overall distribution of student performance well. If the two histograms were very different, this would suggest that the model misses some important structure in the data.

In this graph, the observed and replicated distributions are quite similar. Most students are concentrated in the middle and middle-high range of performance, especially between about 0.50 and 0.75 proportion correct. The replicated data follows the same general shape as the observed data, so the Rasch model seems to capture the overall distribution of student scores reasonably well.

There are still some small differences between the two histograms, but they do not appear very large. This means that the model is not perfect, but it is able to reproduce the main pattern of student performance. This check supports the idea that the Rasch model has an acceptable global fit.

However, this graph only evaluates the overall distribution of student scores. It does not prove that the model fits every single item or every single student perfectly. For this reason, it should be interpreted as a general posterior predictive check, not as a complete proof that the model has no problems.

# Rasch predictive indices

```{r rasch-predictive-indices}
p1_safe <- clip_probs(p_hat_1pl)

log_loss_1pl <- -mean(
  Y * log(p1_safe) + (1 - Y) * log(1 - p1_safe)
)

accuracy_1pl <- mean((p_hat_1pl > 0.5) == Y)

student_rmse_1pl <- rmse(observed_student_score, expected_student_score_1pl)
student_mae_1pl <- mae(observed_student_score, expected_student_score_1pl)

student_correlation_1pl <- cor(
  observed_student_score,
  expected_student_score_1pl,
  use = "complete.obs"
)

item_rmse_1pl <- rmse(observed_item_score, expected_item_score_1pl)
item_mae_1pl <- mae(observed_item_score, expected_item_score_1pl)

item_correlation_1pl <- cor(
  observed_item_score,
  expected_item_score_1pl,
  use = "complete.obs"
)

indices_1pl <- data.frame(
  model = "1PL / Rasch",
  log_loss = log_loss_1pl,
  accuracy = accuracy_1pl,
  student_rmse = student_rmse_1pl,
  student_mae = student_mae_1pl,
  student_correlation = student_correlation_1pl,
  item_rmse = item_rmse_1pl,
  item_mae = item_mae_1pl,
  item_correlation = item_correlation_1pl
)

cat("Predictive accuracy indices for the Rasch model:\n")
cat("Lower log_loss, RMSE, and MAE are better. Higher accuracy, student_correlation, and item_correlation are better.\n")
print(indices_1pl)
```

The predictive accuracy indices evaluate how well the Rasch model reproduces the observed response data. Let $y_{ij}$ be the observed response of student $i$ to item $j$, where $y_{ij}=1$ means a correct answer and $y_{ij}=0$ means an incorrect answer. Let $\hat{p}_{ij}$ be the probability of a correct answer predicted by the Rasch model.

The **log-loss** measures the quality of the predicted probabilities:

$$
\text{Log-loss}
=
-\frac{1}{NJ}
\sum_{i=1}^{N}
\sum_{j=1}^{J}
\left[
y_{ij}\log(\hat{p}_{ij})
+
(1-y_{ij})\log(1-\hat{p}_{ij})
\right]
$$

Lower values of log-loss indicate better probabilistic prediction, because the model assigns higher probability to the responses that actually occurred.

The **accuracy** measures the proportion of individual binary responses correctly classified by the model. A predicted response is classified as correct when $\hat{p}_{ij} \geq 0.5$:

$$
\hat{y}_{ij}
=
\begin{cases}
1 & \text{if } \hat{p}_{ij} \geq 0.5 \\
0 & \text{if } \hat{p}_{ij} < 0.5
\end{cases}
$$

Then, accuracy is computed as:

$$
\text{Accuracy}
=
\frac{1}{NJ}
\sum_{i=1}^{N}
\sum_{j=1}^{J}
\mathbb{I}(\hat{y}_{ij}=y_{ij})
$$

The **student-level RMSE** and **MAE** compare, for each student, the observed proportion of correct answers with the proportion predicted by the model. For student $i$, the observed score is:

$$
s_i = \frac{1}{J}\sum_{j=1}^{J} y_{ij}
$$

while the predicted score is:

$$
\hat{s}_i = \frac{1}{J}\sum_{j=1}^{J} \hat{p}_{ij}
$$

The student-level errors are then computed as:

$$
\text{RMSE}_{student}
=
\sqrt{
\frac{1}{N}
\sum_{i=1}^{N}
(s_i-\hat{s}_i)^2
}
$$

$$
\text{MAE}_{student}
=
\frac{1}{N}
\sum_{i=1}^{N}
|s_i-\hat{s}_i|
$$

Lower values of RMSE and MAE mean that the predicted student scores are close to the observed student scores. The student-level correlation compares $s_i$ and $\hat{s}_i$. A high positive correlation means that students are ranked similarly by their observed performance and by the model predictions.

The same logic is applied at the **item level**. For item $j$, the observed proportion of correct answers is:

$$
q_j = \frac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^{N} y_{ij}
$$

while the average predicted probability of a correct answer is:

$$
\hat{q}_j = \frac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^{N} \hat{p}_{ij}
$$

The item-level RMSE and MAE compare $q_j$ and $\hat{q}_j$ across items:

$$
\text{RMSE}_{item}
=
\sqrt{
\frac{1}{J}
\sum_{j=1}^{J}
(q_j-\hat{q}_j)^2
}
$$

$$
\text{MAE}_{item}
=
\frac{1}{J}
\sum_{j=1}^{J}
|q_j-\hat{q}_j|
$$

The item-level correlation compares the observed and predicted item proportions. A value close to $1$ means that the Rasch model reproduces the ranking of item difficulty very well.

Overall, low RMSE and MAE values, together with high student-level and item-level correlations, suggest that the Rasch model captures the main structure of the observed response data. In our case, these indices indicate good predictive accuracy.

# Model 2: Bayesian 2PL model

The 2PL model is an extension of the 1PL/Rasch model.

In the 1PL model, each item has only one item parameter: the difficulty parameter \(b_j\). This means that items can be easier or more difficult, but all items are assumed to separate students in the same way.

The 2PL model is more flexible because it adds a second item parameter: the discrimination parameter \(a_j\). The model is:

$$
\text{logit}(p_{ij}) = a_j(\theta_i - b_j)
$$

where

$$
\begin{aligned}
\theta_i & = \text{student ability} \\ 
b_j & = \text{item difficulty} \\ 
a_j & = \text{item discrimination}
\end{aligned}
$$

The difficulty parameter \(b_j\) tells us where the item is located on the ability scale. If \(b_j\) is high, the item is more difficult, because a student needs a higher ability level to have a good probability of answering correctly. If \(b_j\) is low, the item is easier.

The discrimination parameter \(a_j\) tells us how well the item separates students with different ability levels. A high value of \(a_j\) means that the item is very informative: students below the item difficulty have a much lower probability of answering correctly, while students above the item difficulty have a much higher probability of answering correctly.

In graphical terms, a high-discrimination item has a steeper item characteristic curve. This means that the probability of a correct answer increases very quickly around the difficulty point. A low-discrimination item has a flatter curve, meaning that weaker and stronger students have more similar probabilities of answering correctly.

This is important because difficulty alone is not enough to judge the quality of an item. An item can be difficult, but not very useful if it does not clearly separate stronger and weaker students. The 2PL model allows us to identify not only which items are easy or difficult, but also which items are more informative for measuring student ability.

In simple terms, the 1PL model asks: “How difficult is this item?”. The 2PL model asks two questions: “How difficult is this item?” and “How well does this item distinguish strong students from weak students?”.

```{r model-2pl, eval=run_2pl}
model_2pl <- "
model {

  for(i in 1:N) {

    theta[i] ~ dnorm(0, 1)

    for(j in 1:J) {

      Y[i, j] ~ dbern(p[i, j])

      logit(p[i, j]) <- a[j] * (theta[i] - b[j])

      log_lik[i, j] <- Y[i, j] * log(p[i, j]) +
                       (1 - Y[i, j]) * log(1 - p[i, j])

      dev_contribution[i, j] <- -2 * log_lik[i, j]

    }

    student_deviance[i] <- sum(dev_contribution[i, 1:J])
  }

  for(j in 1:J) {

    b[j] ~ dnorm(0, 1)

    a[j] ~ dgamma(2, 2)

  }

  total_deviance <- sum(student_deviance[1:N])
}
"

writeLines(model_2pl, "model_2pl.txt")
```

This code specifies the structure of the Bayesian 2PL model. For each student, the model estimates an ability parameter (\theta_i). For each item, it estimates both a difficulty parameter (b_j) and a discrimination parameter (a_j). The observed answer (Y_{ij}) follows a Bernoulli distribution, since the response is correct or incorrect. The logit of the probability of a correct answer is modeled as (a_j(\theta_i - b_j)), meaning that performance depends on the distance between ability and difficulty, weighted by item discrimination.

# Run the 2PL model

```{r inits-2pl, eval=run_2pl}
if(!reuse_existing_results) {
  inits_2pl <- vector("list", n_chains)
  
  for(chain in 1:n_chains) {
    inits_2pl[[chain]] <- list(
      theta = rnorm(N, 0, 0.2),
      b = rnorm(J, 0, 0.2),
      a = rgamma(J, shape = 2, rate = 2)
    )
  }
}
```

Before running the MCMC algorithm, JAGS needs starting values for all unknown parameters. In this case, the code creates initial values for (\theta_i), (b_j), and (a_j). Student abilities and item difficulties are initialized close to zero, while discrimination parameters are initialized from a Gamma distribution, so they are positive.
