This is coherent with the model because discrimination should usually be positive: higher ability should increase the probability of a correct answer.

```{r run-2pl, cache=TRUE, eval=run_2pl}
if(reuse_existing_results) {

  load(file.path(results_dir, "two_model_comparison_results.RData"), envir = .GlobalEnv)
  cat("Reuse mode: 2PL posterior samples loaded from two_model_comparison_results.RData.\n")

} else {

  if(run_dic) {
    load.module("dic")
  }

  jags_2pl <- jags.model(
    file = "model_2pl.txt",
    data = jags_data,
    inits = inits_2pl,
    n.chains = n_chains,
    n.adapt = n_adapt
  )

  update(jags_2pl, burn_in)

  samples_2pl <- coda.samples(
    model = jags_2pl,
    variable.names = c("theta", "b", "a"),
    n.iter = n_iter,
    thin = thin_value
  )

  if(run_dic) {
    deviance_trace_2pl <- coda.samples(
      model = jags_2pl,
      variable.names = c("total_deviance"),
      n.iter = n_iter,
      thin = thin_value
    )

    dic_2pl <- dic.samples(
      model = jags_2pl,
      n.iter = n_iter,
      thin = thin_value,
      type = "pD"
    )
  }
}
```
This section controls the estimation of the 2PL model. If reuse_existing_results is true, the code loads the previously saved results. If not, the model is run again in JAGS. First, JAGS compiles the model and performs adaptation, which helps tune the sampler. Then burn-in iterations are run and discarded, so that the chains can move away from their initial values. Finally, the code collects posterior samples for (\theta), (b), and (a), which represent the Bayesian estimates of student ability, item difficulty, and item discrimination.

# 2PL MCMC diagnostics

```{r diagnostics-2pl, eval=run_2pl}
cat("Posterior summary of the 2PL model parameters:\n")
cat("This table reports posterior means, standard deviations, and credible intervals for theta, b, and a.\n")
cat("theta parameters represent student abilities.\n")
cat("b parameters represent item difficulties.\n")
cat("a parameters represent item discriminations.\n\n")
print(summary(samples_2pl))

diagnostic_params_2pl <- c(
  "b[1]", "b[2]", "b[3]", "b[4]", "b[5]", "b[6]",
  "a[1]", "a[2]", "a[3]", "a[4]", "a[5]", "a[6]",
  "theta[1]", "theta[2]", "theta[3]", "theta[4]", "theta[5]", "theta[6]"
)

cat("\nSelected parameters used for convergence diagnostics:\n")
cat("We do not inspect all theta, b and a parameters in detail because there are many of them.\n")
cat("Instead, we inspect a small set of item difficulties, item discriminations and student abilities.\n\n")
print(diagnostic_params_2pl)

gelman_2pl <- gelman.diag(samples_2pl[, diagnostic_params_2pl])
ess_2pl <- effectiveSize(samples_2pl[, diagnostic_params_2pl])

cat("\nGelman-Rubin convergence diagnostic for selected 2PL parameters:\n")
cat("The Gelman-Rubin diagnostic compares the MCMC chains.\n")
cat("Values close to 1 indicate that the chains have converged to the same posterior distribution.\n")
cat("As a practical rule, values below 1.05 are very good, and values below 1.10 are usually acceptable.\n\n")
print(gelman_2pl)

max_gelman_upper <- max(gelman_2pl$psrf[, "Upper C.I."], na.rm = TRUE)

cat("\nInterpretation of the Gelman-Rubin diagnostic for selected parameters:\n")

if(max_gelman_upper < 1.05) {
  cat("The largest upper confidence interval among selected parameters is", round(max_gelman_upper, 3), ".\n")
  cat("This is very close to 1, so the selected 2PL parameters show very good convergence.\n")
} else if(max_gelman_upper < 1.10) {
  cat("The largest upper confidence interval among selected parameters is", round(max_gelman_upper, 3), ".\n")
  cat("This is acceptable, although convergence should still be checked with traceplots.\n")
} else {
  cat("The largest upper confidence interval among selected parameters is", round(max_gelman_upper, 3), ".\n")
  cat("This may indicate convergence problems. More iterations or better starting values may be needed.\n")
}

cat("\nEffective sample size for selected 2PL parameters:\n")
cat("The effective sample size measures how much independent information is contained in the MCMC draws.\n")
cat("Because MCMC draws are autocorrelated, the effective sample size is usually lower than the total number of saved draws.\n")
cat("Higher values are better.\n\n")
print(round(ess_2pl, 2))

saved_draws_per_chain <- niter(samples_2pl)
number_of_chains <- nchain(samples_2pl)
total_saved_draws <- saved_draws_per_chain * number_of_chains

cat("\nNumber of saved posterior draws:\n")
cat("Saved draws per chain:", saved_draws_per_chain, "\n")
cat("Number of chains:", number_of_chains, "\n")
cat("Total saved draws:", total_saved_draws, "\n")

cat("\nInterpretation of the effective sample size for selected parameters:\n")
cat("The maximum possible effective sample size is approximately equal to the total number of saved draws.\n")
cat("Here, the total number of saved draws is", total_saved_draws, ".\n")
cat("If an effective sample size is close to this value, the MCMC draws contain almost as much information as independent draws.\n")
cat("If an effective sample size is much lower, the chain has more autocorrelation for that parameter.\n\n")

min_ess <- min(ess_2pl, na.rm = TRUE)
max_ess <- max(ess_2pl, na.rm = TRUE)

cat("Minimum effective sample size among selected parameters:", round(min_ess, 2), "\n")
cat("Maximum effective sample size among selected parameters:", round(max_ess, 2), "\n")

if(min_ess > 400) {
  cat("All selected parameters have a good effective sample size for this run.\n")
} else if(min_ess > 200) {
  cat("The effective sample sizes are acceptable.\n")
} else {
  cat("Some effective sample sizes are low. More MCMC iterations may be useful.\n")
}

# General diagnostics for all 2PL parameters

all_params_2pl <- varnames(samples_2pl)

all_params_2pl <- all_params_2pl[
  grepl("^b\\[", all_params_2pl) |
  grepl("^a\\[", all_params_2pl) |
  grepl("^theta\\[", all_params_2pl)
]

cat("\nGeneral diagnostics for all 2PL parameters:\n")
cat("The following diagnostics are computed using all monitored item difficulties, item discriminations and student abilities.\n")
cat("This gives a broader picture than the selected subset used above.\n\n")

gelman_all_2pl <- gelman.diag(
  samples_2pl[, all_params_2pl],
  multivariate = FALSE
)

gelman_all_psrf <- gelman_all_2pl$psrf

upper_ci_all <- gelman_all_psrf[, "Upper C.I."]
upper_ci_all <- upper_ci_all[!is.na(upper_ci_all)]

max_gelman_upper_all <- max(upper_ci_all)
max_gelman_upper_all_param <- names(upper_ci_all)[which.max(upper_ci_all)]

cat("Highest Gelman-Rubin upper confidence interval among all 2PL parameters:\n")
cat(max_gelman_upper_all_param, "=", round(max_gelman_upper_all, 3), "\n")

if(max_gelman_upper_all < 1.05) {
  cat("Overall, the 2PL model shows very good convergence across all monitored parameters.\n")
} else if(max_gelman_upper_all < 1.10) {
  cat("Overall, convergence is acceptable, although some parameters should still be checked carefully.\n")
} else {
  cat("At least one parameter may have convergence problems. More iterations or better starting values may be useful.\n")
}

ess_all_2pl <- effectiveSize(samples_2pl[, all_params_2pl])

ess_all_2pl <- ess_all_2pl[!is.na(ess_all_2pl)]

min_ess_all <- min(ess_all_2pl)
max_ess_all <- max(ess_all_2pl)

min_ess_all_param <- names(ess_all_2pl)[which.min(ess_all_2pl)]
max_ess_all_param <- names(ess_all_2pl)[which.max(ess_all_2pl)]

cat("\nEffective sample size among all 2PL parameters:\n")
cat("Lowest effective sample size:", min_ess_all_param, "=", round(min_ess_all, 2), "\n")
cat("Highest effective sample size:", max_ess_all_param, "=", round(max_ess_all, 2), "\n")

if(min_ess_all > 400) {
  cat("All 2PL parameters have a good effective sample size for this run.\n")
} else if(min_ess_all > 200) {
  cat("The effective sample sizes are generally acceptable, although some parameters are less efficiently sampled.\n")
} else {
  cat("Some 2PL parameters have low effective sample size. More MCMC iterations may be useful.\n")
}
```

In this model, theta represents student ability, b represents item difficulty, and a represents item discrimination. Higher values of theta indicate stronger students, higher values of b indicate more difficult items, and higher values of a indicate items that better discriminate between students with different ability levels.

The Gelman-Rubin diagnostic is used to assess the convergence of the MCMC chains. The basic idea is to compare the variability **within each chain** with the variability **between different chains**. If the chains have converged to the same posterior distribution, these two sources of variability should be similar. For this reason, values of the Potential Scale Reduction Factor, or **PSRF**, close to **1** indicate good convergence.

In this output, the PSRF values for the selected 2PL parameters are all very close to 1, suggesting that the chains mixed well and reached a stable posterior distribution. The upper confidence limits are also close to 1, which reinforces the evidence of convergence.

The multivariate PSRF extends this idea by checking convergence for the selected parameters **jointly**, rather than one parameter at a time. Since the multivariate PSRF is also equal to 1, this provides additional evidence that the MCMC algorithm performed well for the selected parameters. Overall, these diagnostics suggest that there are no clear convergence problems in this part of the 2PL model.


```{r trace-2pl-b, eval=run_2pl}
# These traceplots show the MCMC chains for the first six item difficulty parameters.
# In the 2PL model, b[j] represents the difficulty of item j.
# A good traceplot should fluctuate around a stable region with no long trend.

traceplot(samples_2pl[, c("b[1]", "b[2]", "b[3]", "b[4]", "b[5]", "b[6]")])
```

The traceplots above show the MCMC chains for the first six 2PL item difficulty parameters. They are not all the item difficulties, but a representative subset used for visual convergence checking. If the chains overlap and move around the same stable region, this suggests that the posterior samples for these difficulty parameters are reliable. If the chains are separated or show a trend, the MCMC run may need more iterations.

```{r trace-2pl-a, eval=run_2pl}
# These traceplots show the MCMC chains for the first six item discrimination parameters.
# In the 2PL model, a[j] measures how strongly item j separates weak and strong students.
# Stable and well-mixed chains suggest reliable posterior estimates of discrimination.

traceplot(samples_2pl[, c("a[1]", "a[2]", "a[3]", "a[4]", "a[5]", "a[6]")])
```

The traceplots above show the MCMC chains for the first six item discrimination parameters. This is especially important in the 2PL model because discrimination is the new parameter added compared with the Rasch model. If these chains mix well, it suggests that the model is able to estimate how informative the selected items are. Only six discrimination parameters are shown because plotting all item parameters would make the diagnostic section too long.

```{r trace-2pl-theta, eval=run_2pl}
# These traceplots show the MCMC chains for the first six student ability parameters.
# In the 2PL model, theta[i] represents the latent ability of student i.
# Since there is one theta for each sampled student, we only inspect a small subset.

traceplot(samples_2pl[, c("theta[1]", "theta[2]", "theta[3]", "theta[4]", "theta[5]", "theta[6]")])
```

The traceplots above show the MCMC chains for the first six student ability parameters in the 2PL model. Since the model estimates one `theta` for every sampled student, it is not practical to show all traceplots. The purpose is to check whether a representative subset of ability parameters behaves well. Stable traceplots support the reliability of the posterior ability estimates used in the following graphs.

# 2PL posterior estimates

```{r extract-2pl, eval=run_2pl}
matrix_2pl <- as.matrix(samples_2pl)

theta2_cols <- sort_jags_cols(grep("^theta\\[", colnames(matrix_2pl), value = TRUE))
b2_cols <- sort_jags_cols(grep("^b\\[", colnames(matrix_2pl), value = TRUE))
a2_cols <- sort_jags_cols(grep("^a\\[", colnames(matrix_2pl), value = TRUE))

theta_hat_2pl <- as.numeric(colMeans(matrix_2pl[, theta2_cols, drop = FALSE]))
b_hat_2pl <- as.numeric(colMeans(matrix_2pl[, b2_cols, drop = FALSE]))
a_hat_2pl <- as.numeric(colMeans(matrix_2pl[, a2_cols, drop = FALSE]))

student_score_2pl <- rowMeans(Y)
item_score_2pl <- colMeans(Y)

stopifnot(length(theta_hat_2pl) == nrow(Y))
stopifnot(length(student_score_2pl) == nrow(Y))
stopifnot(length(wle) == nrow(Y))

stopifnot(length(b_hat_2pl) == ncol(Y))
stopifnot(length(a_hat_2pl) == ncol(Y))
stopifnot(length(item_score_2pl) == ncol(Y))

results_students_2pl <- data.frame(
  student_id = sample_id,
  theta_hat_2pl = theta_hat_2pl,
  raw_score = student_score_2pl,
  WLE_ITA_200 = wle
)

results_items_2pl <- data.frame(
  item = colnames(Y),
  b_hat_2pl = b_hat_2pl,
  a_hat_2pl = a_hat_2pl,
  proportion_correct = item_score_2pl
)

cat("First rows of the student-level 2PL results:\n")
cat("Each row is one sampled student, with estimated ability, raw score, and WLE score.\n")
print(head(results_students_2pl))

cat("\nFirst rows of the item-level 2PL results:\n")
cat("Each row is one item, with estimated difficulty, estimated discrimination, and observed proportion correct.\n")
print(head(results_items_2pl))
```

# Interpret 2PL item difficulty

In the 2PL model, item difficulty has the same basic meaning as in the Rasch model: higher `b_hat_2pl` values indicate more difficult items, while lower values indicate easier items. However, the 2PL model also estimates discrimination, so difficulty is interpreted together with how informative each item is.

```{r interpret-2pl-difficulty, eval=run_2pl}
# We add ranking columns and percentage columns to compare item difficulty
# in a model-based way and in a raw-data way.
# For b_hat_2pl, lower values mean easier items.
# For proportion_correct, higher values mean easier items.
# observed_difficulty_percent is the percentage of students who answered incorrectly.

results_items_2pl <- results_items_2pl %>%
  mutate(
    observed_easiness_percent = proportion_correct * 100,
    observed_difficulty_percent = (1 - proportion_correct) * 100,
    rank_b_hat_easy_to_hard = rank(b_hat_2pl, ties.method = "first"),
    rank_prop_easy_to_hard = rank(-proportion_correct, ties.method = "first"),
    rank_difference = rank_b_hat_easy_to_hard - rank_prop_easy_to_hard,
    abs_rank_difference = abs(rank_difference)
  )

results_items_2pl_difficulty <- results_items_2pl %>%
  arrange(rank_b_hat_easy_to_hard)

cor_b2_itemscore <- cor(
  results_items_2pl$b_hat_2pl,
  results_items_2pl$proportion_correct
)

cor_rank_b2_prop <- cor(
  results_items_2pl$rank_b_hat_easy_to_hard,
  results_items_2pl$rank_prop_easy_to_hard,
  method = "spearman"
)

cat("Correlation between 2PL difficulty and observed item proportion correct:\n")
cat("This value should usually be negative: difficult items should have lower proportions correct.\n")
print(cor_b2_itemscore)

cat("\nSpearman correlation between the two item difficulty rankings:\n")
cat("This value should usually be positive and high if the two rankings are similar.\n")
print(cor_rank_b2_prop)

cat("\nItem-level 2PL results ordered from easiest to hardest according to b_hat_2pl:\n")
print(
  results_items_2pl_difficulty %>%
    select(
      item,
      b_hat_2pl,
      a_hat_2pl,
      proportion_correct,
      observed_easiness_percent,
      observed_difficulty_percent,
      rank_b_hat_easy_to_hard,
      rank_prop_easy_to_hard,
      rank_difference,
      abs_rank_difference
    )
)


rank_diagnostics_2pl <- results_items_2pl %>%
  summarise(
    number_of_items = n(),
    mean_abs_rank_difference = mean(abs_rank_difference),
    median_abs_rank_difference = median(abs_rank_difference),
    max_abs_rank_difference = max(abs_rank_difference),
    items_with_same_rank = sum(abs_rank_difference == 0),
    items_with_difference_1_or_less = sum(abs_rank_difference <= 1),
    items_with_difference_3_or_less = sum(abs_rank_difference <= 3),
    items_with_difference_5_or_less = sum(abs_rank_difference <= 5)
  )

print(rank_diagnostics_2pl)

cat("\nItems with the largest ranking differences:\n")
print(
  results_items_2pl %>%
    arrange(desc(abs_rank_difference)) %>%
    select(
      item,
      b_hat_2pl,
      a_hat_2pl,
      proportion_correct,
      rank_b_hat_easy_to_hard,
      rank_prop_easy_to_hard,
      rank_difference,
      abs_rank_difference
    ) %>%
    head(10)
)
```

The table compares the 2PL model-based difficulty ranking with the raw observed difficulty ranking. The column `rank_b_hat_easy_to_hard` orders items using the posterior mean 2PL difficulty, while `rank_prop_easy_to_hard` orders items using the observed proportion correct. The percentage columns make the table easier to read: `observed_easiness_percent` is the percentage of correct answers, while `observed_difficulty_percent` is the percentage of wrong answers.

About the Spearman correlation, it compares the ranking of items obtained from the 2PL model with the ranking obtained from the observed proportion of correct answers. Unlike a standard correlation, it does not compare the exact numerical values, but only the order of the items. In this case, the value is very high and positive, meaning that the two rankings are very similar. Therefore, the items estimated as easier by the 2PL model are generally also the items with a higher observed proportion of correct answers, while the items estimated as harder are generally those with a lower observed proportion of correct answers.

```{r plot-2pl-difficulty, eval=run_2pl}
eps_2pl <- 1e-6

plot_items_2pl_difficulty <- results_items_2pl_difficulty %>%
  mutate(
    proportion_correct_safe_2pl = pmin(pmax(proportion_correct, eps_2pl), 1 - eps_2pl),
    observed_difficulty_from_prop_2pl = -qlogis(proportion_correct_safe_2pl),
    difficulty_difference_2pl = b_hat_2pl - observed_difficulty_from_prop_2pl
  ) %>%
  arrange(b_hat_2pl) %>%
  mutate(item_ordered = factor(item, levels = item))

plot_items_2pl_difficulty_long <- plot_items_2pl_difficulty %>%
  select(item_ordered, b_hat_2pl, observed_difficulty_from_prop_2pl) %>%
  pivot_longer(
    cols = c(b_hat_2pl, observed_difficulty_from_prop_2pl),
    names_to = "measure",
    values_to = "difficulty_value"
  ) %>%
  mutate(
    measure = recode(
      measure,
      b_hat_2pl = "Bayesian 2PL difficulty",
      observed_difficulty_from_prop_2pl = "Observed difficulty from proportion correct"
    )
  )

difference_labels_2pl <- plot_items_2pl_difficulty %>%
  mutate(
    measure = "Bayesian 2PL difficulty",
    difference_label = round(difficulty_difference_2pl, 2)
  )

ggplot(plot_items_2pl_difficulty_long, aes(x = item_ordered, y = difficulty_value, fill = measure)) +
  geom_col(position = position_dodge(width = 0.8), width = 0.7) +
  geom_text(
    data = difference_labels_2pl,
    aes(
      x = item_ordered,
      y = b_hat_2pl,
      label = difference_label,
      group = measure
    ),
    position = position_dodge(width = 0.8),
    hjust = ifelse(difference_labels_2pl$b_hat_2pl >= 0, -0.1, 1.1),
    size = 2.8,
    inherit.aes = FALSE
  ) +
  coord_flip() +
  scale_y_continuous(expand = expansion(mult = c(0.15, 0.25))) +
  labs(
    title = "Bayesian 2PL difficulty compared with observed item difficulty",
    subtitle = "Text labels show the numerical difference: Bayesian 2PL difficulty minus observed difficulty",
    x = "Item",
    y = "Difficulty value",
    fill = "Measure"
  ) +
  theme(
    legend.position = "bottom"
  )
```
**Interpretation of the 2PL difficulty graph.**
This graph compares the posterior mean difficulty estimated by the Bayesian 2PL model with an observed difficulty index derived from the proportion of correct answers. The observed proportion was transformed using `-logit(proportion_correct)`, so both measures have the same interpretation: lower values indicate easier items, while higher values indicate more difficult items.

As already seen in the Rasch/1PL difficulty graph, the model-based difficulty estimates are broadly consistent with the observed difficulty: items answered correctly by many students usually receive lower difficulty values, while items answered correctly by fewer students usually receive higher difficulty values. However, in the 2PL graph the differences between the Bayesian difficulty and the observed difficulty are larger than in the 1PL graph. This is normal, because the Rasch model estimates only item difficulty and assumes the same discrimination for all items, while the 2PL model estimates both item difficulty and item discrimination.

For this reason, the 2PL difficulty is not based only on how many students answered each item correctly, but also on which students answered correctly. If an item separates high-ability and low-ability students differently from the others, the 2PL model can adjust its difficulty estimate, producing a larger difference from the simple observed difficulty based on the proportion correct.

Therefore, these differences should not automatically be interpreted as errors. They show that the 2PL model is using more information than the raw percentage of correct answers. In particular, the 2PL model can move some item difficulties farther from the observed difficulty because it also considers the discrimination power of each item.


```{r plot-2pl-item-difficulty-scatter, eval=run_2pl, fig.width=10, fig.height=7}
ggplot(results_items_2pl, aes(x = proportion_correct, y = b_hat_2pl, label = item)) +
  geom_point(size = 2) +
  geom_smooth(method = "lm", se = FALSE) +
  ggrepel::geom_label_repel(
    size = 3,
    max.overlaps = Inf,
    box.padding = 0.35,
    point.padding = 0.25,
    min.segment.length = 0,
    segment.size = 0.3
  ) +
  labs(
    title = "Observed item performance vs 2PL difficulty",
    x = "Observed proportion correct",
    y = "Posterior mean difficulty from 2PL"
  ) +
  theme(
    plot.margin = margin(10, 20, 10, 10)
  )
```

**Interpretation of the observed item performance vs 2PL difficulty graph.**  
This scatter plot compares the observed proportion correct with the posterior mean difficulty estimated by the 2PL model. As in the Rasch model, a negative relationship is expected: items answered correctly by many students should usually have lower estimated difficulty, while items answered correctly by fewer students should usually have higher estimated difficulty.

In the 2PL model, however, this relationship can be slightly less mechanical than in the Rasch model because difficulty is estimated together with item discrimination. This means that the model does not only consider how many students answered each item correctly, but also how strongly each item separates students with different ability levels. For this reason, the graph is useful as a visual check that the 2PL difficulty estimates remain coherent with the raw item performance, while still allowing some adjustment due to discrimination.

Anyways, the points follow a clear decreasing pattern, meaning the model-based difficulty estimates are coherent with the raw item performance. Items that deviate more from the general pattern are not necessarily wrong estimates; they may be items where the response pattern contains additional information that is captured by the 2PL model through the discrimination parameter.

# Interpret 2PL item discrimination

```{r interpret-2pl-discrimination, eval=run_2pl}
# We add ranking columns to analyse item discrimination.
# For a_hat_2pl, higher values mean more discriminating items.
# For proportion_correct, higher values mean easier items.
# Important: proportion_correct measures item easiness, not discrimination.
# Therefore, the correlation between a_hat_2pl and proportion_correct is only descriptive.

results_items_2pl <- results_items_2pl %>%
  mutate(
    observed_easiness_percent = proportion_correct * 100,
    observed_difficulty_percent = (1 - proportion_correct) * 100,
    rank_a_hat_most_to_least_discriminating = rank(-a_hat_2pl, ties.method = "first"),
    rank_prop_easy_to_hard = rank(-proportion_correct, ties.method = "first"),
    rank_difference_discrimination_vs_easiness =
      rank_a_hat_most_to_least_discriminating - rank_prop_easy_to_hard,
    abs_rank_difference_discrimination_vs_easiness =
      abs(rank_difference_discrimination_vs_easiness)
  )

results_items_2pl_discrimination <- results_items_2pl %>%
  arrange(rank_a_hat_most_to_least_discriminating)

cor_a2_itemscore <- cor(
  results_items_2pl$a_hat_2pl,
  results_items_2pl$proportion_correct
)

cor_rank_a2_prop <- cor(
  results_items_2pl$rank_a_hat_most_to_least_discriminating,
  results_items_2pl$rank_prop_easy_to_hard,
  method = "spearman"
)

cat("Correlation between 2PL discrimination and observed item proportion correct:\n")
cat("This value is only descriptive, because proportion correct measures easiness, not discrimination.\n")
print(cor_a2_itemscore)



cat("\nItem-level 2PL results ordered from most to least discriminating according to a_hat_2pl:\n")
print(
  results_items_2pl_discrimination %>%
    select(
      item,
      a_hat_2pl,
      b_hat_2pl,
      proportion_correct,
      observed_easiness_percent,
      observed_difficulty_percent,
      rank_a_hat_most_to_least_discriminating,
      rank_prop_easy_to_hard,
      rank_difference_discrimination_vs_easiness,
      abs_rank_difference_discrimination_vs_easiness
    )
)

rank_diagnostics_2pl_discrimination <- results_items_2pl %>%
  summarise(
    number_of_items = n(),
    mean_abs_rank_difference = mean(abs_rank_difference_discrimination_vs_easiness),
    median_abs_rank_difference = median(abs_rank_difference_discrimination_vs_easiness),
    max_abs_rank_difference = max(abs_rank_difference_discrimination_vs_easiness),
    items_with_same_rank = sum(abs_rank_difference_discrimination_vs_easiness == 0),
    items_with_difference_1_or_less = sum(abs_rank_difference_discrimination_vs_easiness <= 1),
    items_with_difference_3_or_less = sum(abs_rank_difference_discrimination_vs_easiness <= 3),
    items_with_difference_5_or_less = sum(abs_rank_difference_discrimination_vs_easiness <= 5)
  )

cat("\nSpearman correlation between the discrimination ranking and the observed easiness ranking:\n")
cat("This value may be lower than the difficulty ranking correlation, because discrimination and easiness are different item properties.\n")
print(cor_rank_a2_prop)

cat("\nRanking diagnostics for discrimination versus observed easiness:\n")
print(rank_diagnostics_2pl_discrimination)

cat("\nItems with the largest ranking differences between discrimination and observed easiness:\n")
print(
  results_items_2pl %>%
    arrange(desc(abs_rank_difference_discrimination_vs_easiness)) %>%
    select(
      item,
      a_hat_2pl,
      b_hat_2pl,
      proportion_correct,
      rank_a_hat_most_to_least_discriminating,
      rank_prop_easy_to_hard,
      rank_difference_discrimination_vs_easiness,
      abs_rank_difference_discrimination_vs_easiness
    ) %>%
    head(10)
)

cat("\nTen most discriminating items according to the 2PL model:\n")
print(head(results_items_2pl_discrimination, 10))

cat("\nTen least discriminating items according to the 2PL model:\n")
print(tail(results_items_2pl_discrimination, 10))
```
The table compares the 2PL model-based discrimination ranking with the raw observed easiness ranking. The column `rank_a_hat_most_to_least_discriminating` orders items using the posterior mean 2PL discrimination parameter, while `rank_prop_easy_to_hard` orders items using the observed proportion correct. However, these two rankings measure different aspects of the items. The 2PL discrimination parameter `a_hat_2pl` measures how well an item separates stronger and weaker students, while the observed proportion correct mainly measures how easy or difficult the item was.

About the Spearman correlation, it compares the order of the items according to discrimination with the order of the items according to observed easiness. In this case, the value is about 0.23, which indicates only a weak positive relationship between the two rankings. This result is reasonable, because an item can be very discriminating without necessarily being among the easiest or hardest items. Therefore, unlike the difficulty analysis, we should not expect a very strong correlation between `a_hat_2pl` and the observed proportion correct. The low Spearman value suggests that item discrimination provides additional information that is not captured by the simple percentage of correct answers.


```{r plot-2pl-discrimination, eval=run_2pl}
plot_items_2pl_discrimination <- results_items_2pl_discrimination %>%
  mutate(
    discrimination_label = round(a_hat_2pl, 2)
  )

ggplot(plot_items_2pl_discrimination, aes(x = reorder(item, a_hat_2pl), y = a_hat_2pl)) +
  geom_col() +
  geom_text(
    aes(label = discrimination_label),
    hjust = -0.1,
    size = 3
  ) +
  coord_flip() +
  scale_y_continuous(expand = expansion(mult = c(0, 0.20))) +
  labs(
    title = "Estimated item discrimination from Bayesian 2PL model",
    subtitle = "Text labels show the posterior mean discrimination for each item",
    x = "Item",
    y = "Posterior mean discrimination",
    caption = "Items at the top are the most informative; items at the bottom discriminate less between students."
  )
```

**Interpretation of the discrimination graph.**  
This graph shows the estimated **item discrimination parameter** from the Bayesian 2PL model. In the 2PL model, discrimination measures how strongly an item distinguishes between students with different ability levels. The numerical labels show the posterior mean discrimination for each item. Items with a high discrimination value are more informative because the probability of a correct answer changes more sharply as ability increases. Items with a low discrimination value are less useful for separating students, because their probability curve is flatter. Therefore, the items at the top of the graph are the most discriminating and are expected to have higher information peaks in the item information curves.

This graph is one of the most important additions of the 2PL model compared with the Rasch model. In the Rasch model, all items are assumed to have the same discrimination, so all items are assumed to separate students equally well. In the 2PL model, instead, each item receives its own discrimination parameter.

Items with high discrimination are especially useful because they create a sharper distinction between students with lower and higher ability. This means that a small increase in ability produces a large increase in the probability of answering correctly. Items with low discrimination are less informative because weak and strong students have more similar probabilities of answering correctly. For this reason, low-discrimination items may be less useful for measuring ability, even if they are easy or difficult.

# 2PL item characteristic curves

Item characteristic curves are especially useful for the 2PL model because they show both difficulty and discrimination at the same time. A curve shifted to the right indicates a more difficult item, while a steeper curve indicates a more discriminating item.

```{r plot-2pl-icc-selected-items-big, eval=run_2pl, fig.width=9, fig.height=6, out.width="100%", fig.show="asis", message=FALSE, warning=FALSE}
# Select representative 2PL items.
# The same item can appear more than once if it has more than one role.

average_b_2pl <- mean(results_items_2pl$b_hat_2pl, na.rm = TRUE)

selected_items_2pl <- bind_rows(
  results_items_2pl %>%
    slice_min(b_hat_2pl, n = 1, with_ties = FALSE) %>%
    mutate(type = "Easiest item"),
  results_items_2pl %>%
    slice_max(b_hat_2pl, n = 1, with_ties = FALSE) %>%
    mutate(type = "Hardest item"),
  results_items_2pl %>%
    slice_min(abs(b_hat_2pl - average_b_2pl), n = 1, with_ties = FALSE) %>%
    mutate(type = "Average difficulty item"),
  results_items_2pl %>%
    slice_max(a_hat_2pl, n = 1, with_ties = FALSE) %>%
    mutate(type = "Most discriminating item"),
  results_items_2pl %>%
    slice_min(a_hat_2pl, n = 1, with_ties = FALSE) %>%
    mutate(type = "Least discriminating item")
) %>%
  mutate(
    type = factor(
      type,
      levels = c(
        "Most discriminating item",
        "Easiest item",
        "Average difficulty item",
        "Hardest item",
        "Least discriminating item"
      )
    ),
    item_label = paste0(item, " - ", type),
    difficulty_label = paste0("b = ", round(b_hat_2pl, 2))
  ) %>%
  arrange(type)

theta_grid_2pl <- seq(-4, 4, length.out = 200)

icc_data_2pl <- selected_items_2pl %>%
  tidyr::crossing(theta = theta_grid_2pl) %>%
  mutate(
    probability_correct = plogis(a_hat_2pl * (theta - b_hat_2pl))
  )

for (lab in selected_items_2pl$item_label) {
  
  plot_data <- icc_data_2pl %>%
    filter(item_label == lab)
  
  item_info <- selected_items_2pl %>%
    filter(item_label == lab) %>%
    slice(1)
  
  p <- ggplot(plot_data, aes(x = theta, y = probability_correct)) +
    geom_line(linewidth = 1) +
    geom_hline(yintercept = 0.5, linetype = "dashed") +
    geom_vline(
      xintercept = item_info$b_hat_2pl,
      linetype = "dashed"
    ) +
    geom_point(
      data = item_info,
      aes(x = b_hat_2pl, y = 0.5),
      inherit.aes = FALSE,
      size = 2
    ) +
    geom_text(
      data = item_info,
      aes(x = b_hat_2pl, y = 0.55, label = difficulty_label),
      inherit.aes = FALSE,
      angle = 90,
      vjust = -0.5,
      size = 3
    ) +
    labs(
      title = lab,
      subtitle = "2PL item characteristic curve: dashed lines show the difficulty point where P(correct) = 0.50",
      x = "Student ability",
      y = "Probability of correct answer"
    ) +
    coord_cartesian(ylim = c(0, 1))
  
  print(p)
}
```



**Interpretation of the 2PL item characteristic curves.**  
These curves show how the probability of answering an item correctly changes as student ability increases. In the 2PL model, each item has its own **difficulty** parameter and its own **discrimination** parameter. The difficulty determines where the curve is located on the ability scale, while the discrimination determines how steep the curve is.

In the graph, **A2** is the most discriminating item. Its curve is very steep: the probability of a correct answer changes very quickly when student ability moves around the central part of the curve. This means that A2 is especially useful for separating students with different ability levels. Students below a certain ability level have a very low probability of answering correctly, while students just above that level have a much higher probability. Therefore, small differences in ability lead to large differences in the predicted probability of success.

**A3** is the easiest item. Its curve is shifted to the left, meaning that even students with relatively low ability already have a fairly good probability of answering correctly. The probability increases quickly and then becomes very close to 1 for medium and high ability students. This means that A3 is useful for identifying very low-ability students, but it becomes less informative among medium and high-ability students because almost all of them are predicted to answer correctly.

**B10** is the hardest and less  discriminating item. Its curve is shifted to the right and remains much lower than the others across most of the ability range. Even students with high ability do not reach a probability close to 1. This means that B10 is a difficult item, because students need a much higher ability level to have a strong probability of answering correctly. Moreover the probability of a correct answer increases only slowly as student ability increases. In practical terms, this item does not separate students very clearly, because low, medium, and high-ability students have probabilities that are not extremely different from each other.

Compared with the Rasch model, the 2PL model is more flexible because the item curves are not forced to have the same steepness. In the Rasch model, items differ only in difficulty, so the curves are shifted left or right but have the same general shape. In the 2PL model, instead, items can differ both in difficulty and discrimination. This is why A2 has a very steep curve, A3 is easier and shifted to the left, while B10 is difficult and much flatter.


# 2PL item information curves

Item information curves show which items are most useful for estimating ability at different points of the ability scale. This is one of the clearest advantages of the 2PL model, because items with higher discrimination can provide more information.

```{r plot-2pl-information-selected-items-big, eval=run_2pl, fig.width=9, fig.height=6, out.width="100%", fig.show="asis", message=FALSE, warning=FALSE}
# Item information shows how useful an item is at different ability levels.
# In the 2PL model, highly discriminating items usually provide more information.
# The peak is around the item difficulty, while the height depends on discrimination.

information_data_2pl <- selected_items_2pl %>%
  tidyr::crossing(theta = theta_grid_2pl) %>%
  mutate(
    probability_correct = plogis(a_hat_2pl * (theta - b_hat_2pl)),
    item_information = a_hat_2pl^2 * probability_correct * (1 - probability_correct),
    item_label = paste0(item, " - ", type)
  )

difficulty_labels_2pl <- selected_items_2pl %>%
  mutate(
    item_label = paste0(item, " - ", type),
    difficulty_label = paste0(
      "b = ", round(b_hat_2pl, 2),
      "\n",
      "a = ", round(a_hat_2pl, 2)
    )
  )

information_peak_labels_2pl <- information_data_2pl %>%
  group_by(item_label) %>%
  summarise(label_y = max(item_information), .groups = "drop") %>%
  left_join(difficulty_labels_2pl, by = "item_label")

for (lab in selected_items_2pl$item_label) {
  
  plot_data <- information_data_2pl %>%
    filter(item_label == lab)
  
  difficulty_data <- difficulty_labels_2pl %>%
    filter(item_label == lab)
  
  label_data <- information_peak_labels_2pl %>%
    filter(item_label == lab)
  
  p <- ggplot(plot_data, aes(x = theta, y = item_information)) +
    geom_line(linewidth = 1) +
    geom_vline(
      data = difficulty_data,
      aes(xintercept = b_hat_2pl),
      linetype = "dashed",
      inherit.aes = FALSE
    ) +
    geom_text(
      data = label_data,
      aes(
        x = b_hat_2pl,
        y = label_y,
        label = difficulty_label
      ),
      angle = 90,
      vjust = -0.4,
      hjust = 1.1,
      size = 4,
      inherit.aes = FALSE
    ) +
    labs(
      title = lab,
      subtitle = "2PL item information curve",
      x = "Student ability",
      y = "Item information"
    )
  
  print(p)
}
```

**Interpretation of the 2PL item information curves.**  
In the 2PL model, item information is calculated as (a^2 \cdot p \cdot (1-p)), where (p) is the predicted probability of answering correctly and (a) is the item discrimination parameter.

This graph shows how much information each selected item provides for estimating student ability at different points of the ability scale. The dashed vertical line shows the estimated difficulty of the item. The peak of the curve is located around this difficulty value, meaning that each item is most informative for students whose ability is close to the item's difficulty.

Unlike the Rasch model, the 2PL model allows each item to have its own discrimination parameter. For this reason, the height of the information curve is important. Items with higher discrimination have higher information peaks and provide more precise information about student ability. Items with lower discrimination have flatter and lower information curves, meaning that they are less useful for separating students with different ability levels.

Therefore, in the Rasch model the most important aspect of the information curve is mainly where the peak is located. In the 2PL model, both aspects matter: where the peak is located tells us the ability level for which the item is most useful, while the height of the peak tells us how informative the item is at its best point.

This graph shows why the 2PL model can be more flexible than the Rasch model. In the Rasch model, information curves mainly differ in their horizontal position, because all items have the same discrimination. In the 2PL model, instead, information curves can also differ in height. This means that some items are not only targeted at different ability levels, but are also much more informative than others.

The most informative items are those with higher discrimination, because their probability of correct answer changes more sharply around the difficulty point. Low-discrimination items have flatter information curves and provide less precise information about student ability. Therefore, this graph helps identify not only where each item is useful on the ability scale, but also how useful it is.


As you can see also here, the hardest and the least discriminatory items are the same items (that is why the graph is the same).

# 2PL difficulty and discrimination together

The next graph compares the two item parameters estimated by the 2PL model. 

```{r plot-2pl-difficulty-discrimination, eval=run_2pl, fig.width=10, fig.height=7}
ggplot(results_items_2pl, aes(x = b_hat_2pl, y = a_hat_2pl, label = item)) +
  geom_point(alpha = 0.7, size = 2) +
  geom_label_repel(
    size = 3,
    max.overlaps = Inf,
    box.padding = 0.35,
    point.padding = 0.25,
    min.segment.length = 0,
    segment.size = 0.3
  ) +
  labs(
    title = "Relationship between item difficulty and item discrimination",
    subtitle = "This graph shows whether difficult items are also good at separating students",
    x = "Posterior mean difficulty",
    y = "Posterior mean discrimination"
  ) +
  coord_cartesian(clip = "off") +
  theme(
    plot.margin = margin(10, 30, 10, 10)
  )
```

**Interpretation of the difficulty-discrimination graph.**  
This graph compares the two main item parameters of the 2PL model. The x-axis shows item difficulty, while the y-axis shows item discrimination. Items on the right are more difficult, while items on the left are easier. Items higher on the graph are more discriminating, meaning that they are better at separating students with different ability levels. The best items are usually those with reasonably high discrimination, because they provide more useful information about student ability. A difficult item is not necessarily a good item: if its discrimination is low, it may be hard but not very informative.

This graph is useful because it separates two concepts that are often confused: item difficulty and item quality. A difficult item is not necessarily a good measurement item. If an item is very difficult but has low discrimination, it may be hard for students but not very useful for distinguishing between different ability levels (a clear example of this are the items b10 and a10).

Ideally, good items should have reasonably high discrimination. Depending on the test purpose, an item can be easy, medium, or difficult, but it should still help separate students. Items with high discrimination and medium difficulty are often especially useful because they provide information for many students around the central part of the ability distribution. Very easy or very difficult items can still be useful, but mainly for students at the extremes of the ability scale.

# Validate 2PL ability with WLE

```{r validate-2pl-wle, eval=run_2pl}
cor_theta2_wle <- cor(
  results_students_2pl$theta_hat_2pl,
  results_students_2pl$WLE_ITA_200,
  use = "complete.obs"
)

cor_theta2_raw <- cor(
  results_students_2pl$theta_hat_2pl,
  results_students_2pl$raw_score,
  use = "complete.obs"
)

cat("Correlation between 2PL ability estimate and WLE_ITA_200:\n")
cat("A positive value means that the Bayesian ability estimate is coherent with the official INVALSI score.\n")
print(cor_theta2_wle)

cat("\nCorrelation between 2PL ability estimate and raw proportion correct:\n")
cat("A high positive value is expected because both variables measure student performance.\n")
print(cor_theta2_raw)
```

```{r plot-2pl-wle, eval=run_2pl}
ggplot(results_students_2pl, aes(x = theta_hat_2pl, y = WLE_ITA_200)) +
  geom_point(alpha = 0.4) +
  geom_smooth(method = "lm", se = FALSE) +
  labs(
    title = "2PL ability estimate vs official WLE score",
    subtitle = "The official WLE score is used only as an external validation benchmark",
    x = "Posterior mean theta from 2PL",
    y = "WLE_ITA_200"
  )
```

**Interpretation of the 2PL ability-WLE graph.**  
This graph compares the Bayesian ability estimated by the 2PL model with the official INVALSI `WLE_ITA_200` score. The positive linear pattern shows that students with higher Bayesian ability estimates also tend to have higher official WLE scores. We can also notice that most students are concentrated in the central part of the graph, while there are fewer students with very low ability and fewer students with very high ability. This is normal in educational data: most students are around the middle, while extreme performances are less frequent.

```{r plot-2pl-raw, eval=run_2pl}
ggplot(results_students_2pl, aes(x = theta_hat_2pl, y = raw_score)) +
  geom_point(alpha = 0.4) +
  geom_smooth(method = "lm", se = FALSE) +
  labs(
    title = "2PL ability estimate vs raw proportion correct",
    subtitle = "Students with higher estimated ability should also have higher observed scores",
    x = "Posterior mean theta from 2PL",
    y = "Raw proportion correct"
  )
```

**Interpretation of the 2PL ability-raw score graph.**  
This graph compares the posterior mean ability estimated by the 2PL model with the raw proportion of correct answers for each student. A strong positive relationship is expected because both variables measure student performance. However, the two measures are not exactly the same. The raw score is a simple proportion of correct answers, while the 2PL ability estimate also accounts for item difficulty and item discrimination.

```{r plot-2pl-theta-distribution-binned, eval=run_2pl}
# This plot shows the distribution of estimated student abilities in the 2PL model.
# It is the 2PL version of the Rasch ability distribution plot.
# The bars show how many students fall inside each theta interval.
# The labels above the bars show the percentage of students in each interval.

theta_breaks_2pl <- seq(-4, 4, by = 0.18)

results_students_2pl_binned <- results_students_2pl %>%
  mutate(
    theta_bin = cut(
      theta_hat_2pl,
      breaks = theta_breaks_2pl,
      include.lowest = TRUE,
      right = FALSE
    )
  ) %>%
  filter(!is.na(theta_bin)) %>%
  count(theta_bin) %>%
  mutate(
    percentage = n / sum(n) * 100,
    percentage_label = paste0(round(percentage, 1), "%")
  )

ggplot(results_students_2pl_binned, aes(x = theta_bin, y = n)) +
  geom_col() +
  geom_text(
    aes(label = percentage_label),
    vjust = -0.3,
    size = 3
  ) +
  labs(
    title = "Distribution of students by estimated 2PL ability",
    subtitle = "Most students are expected to be concentrated around the central ability levels",
    x = "Posterior mean theta interval",
    y = "Number of students"
  ) +
  scale_y_continuous(
    expand = expansion(mult = c(0, 0.12))
  ) +
  theme(
    axis.text.x = element_text(angle = 90, vjust = 0.5, hjust = 1)
  )
```

**Interpretation of the 2PL ability distribution.**  
This bar plot shows how the estimated 2PL abilities are distributed across students. Each bar represents a range of theta values, and the height of the bar tells us how many students fall in that range. The graph helps us see the shape of the student population. Usually, many students are concentrated around the middle of the ability scale, while very weak and very strong students are less frequent. This complements the WLE scatterplot: the scatterplot validates theta against an external score, while this bar plot shows the internal distribution of estimated ability.

# Compare 1PL and 2PL estimates

```{r compare-1pl-2pl, eval=run_2pl}
student_score_compare <- rowMeans(Y)
item_score_compare <- colMeans(Y)

theta_hat_1pl <- as.numeric(theta_hat_1pl)
theta_hat_2pl <- as.numeric(theta_hat_2pl)
b_hat_1pl <- as.numeric(b_hat_1pl)
b_hat_2pl <- as.numeric(b_hat_2pl)
a_hat_2pl <- as.numeric(a_hat_2pl)

stopifnot(length(sample_id) == nrow(Y))
stopifnot(length(theta_hat_1pl) == nrow(Y))
stopifnot(length(theta_hat_2pl) == nrow(Y))
stopifnot(length(student_score_compare) == nrow(Y))
stopifnot(length(wle) == nrow(Y))

stopifnot(length(b_hat_1pl) == ncol(Y))
stopifnot(length(b_hat_2pl) == ncol(Y))
stopifnot(length(a_hat_2pl) == ncol(Y))
stopifnot(length(item_score_compare) == ncol(Y))

results_compare_students <- data.frame(
  student_id = sample_id,
  theta_hat_1pl = theta_hat_1pl,
  theta_hat_2pl = theta_hat_2pl,
  raw_score = student_score_compare,
  WLE_ITA_200 = wle
)

cor_theta_1pl_2pl <- cor(
  results_compare_students$theta_hat_1pl,
  results_compare_students$theta_hat_2pl,
  use = "complete.obs"
)

cat("Correlation between student ability estimates from 1PL and 2PL:\n")
cat("A high positive value means that the two models rank students similarly.\n")
print(cor_theta_1pl_2pl)

results_compare_items <- data.frame(
  item = colnames(Y),
  b_hat_1pl = b_hat_1pl,
  b_hat_2pl = b_hat_2pl,
  a_hat_2pl = a_hat_2pl,
  proportion_correct = item_score_compare
)

cor_b_1pl_2pl <- cor(
  results_compare_items$b_hat_1pl,
  results_compare_items$b_hat_2pl,
  use = "complete.obs"
)

cat("\nCorrelation between item difficulty estimates from 1PL and 2PL:\n")
cat("A high positive value means that the two models rank item difficulty similarly.\n")
print(cor_b_1pl_2pl)
```

```{r plot-theta-compare, eval=run_2pl}
ggplot(results_compare_students, aes(x = theta_hat_1pl, y = theta_hat_2pl)) +
  geom_point(alpha = 0.4) +
  geom_smooth(method = "lm", se = FALSE) +
  labs(
    title = "Student ability estimates: 1PL vs 2PL",
    x = "Posterior mean theta from 1PL",
    y = "Posterior mean theta from 2PL"
  )
```

**Interpretation of the 1PL-2PL ability comparison graph.**  
This graph compares the student ability estimates produced by the Rasch model and by the 2PL model. Each point is one student. If the points follow a clear increasing pattern, the two models rank students in a similar way. Differences are still possible because the 2PL model also considers item discrimination, while the Rasch model assumes the same discrimination for all items.
As you can see in the graph, they are basiclaly the same.

```{r plot-b-compare, eval=run_2pl, fig.width=10, fig.height=7}
ggplot(results_compare_items, aes(x = b_hat_1pl, y = b_hat_2pl, label = item)) +
  geom_point(alpha = 0.7, size = 2) +
  geom_smooth(method = "lm", se = FALSE) +
  geom_label_repel(
    size = 3,
    max.overlaps = Inf,
    box.padding = 0.35,
    point.padding = 0.25,
    min.segment.length = 0,
    segment.size = 0.3
  ) +
  labs(
    title = "Item difficulty estimates: 1PL vs 2PL",
    subtitle = "Each label identifies the corresponding item",
    x = "Posterior mean difficulty from 1PL",
    y = "Posterior mean difficulty from 2PL"
  ) +
  coord_cartesian(clip = "off") +
  theme(
    plot.margin = margin(10, 30, 10, 10)
  )
```

**Interpretation of the 1PL-2PL difficulty comparison graph.**  
This graph compares the difficulty estimated for each item by the Rasch model and by the 2PL model. Each point is one item. A strong positive pattern means that the two models generally agree about which items are easier and which items are harder. However, the 2PL estimates can change because the model also estimates discrimination, so item difficulty is adjusted together with how informative the item is (that is why for example there are points such as a10 and b10).

# 2PL posterior predictive check
In this posterior predictive check, we simulated a new artificial response matrix from the fitted 2PL model. First, the model estimated the probability that each student answers each item correctly, using the posterior estimates of ability, difficulty, and discrimination. Then, using these predicted probabilities, we generated a replicated sample of binary responses, where each value is either 0 or 1. This replicated dataset represents what we would expect to observe if the fitted 2PL model were the true data-generating process. Finally, we compared the observed proportions with both the predicted probabilities and the replicated proportions. The fact that these values are generally close suggests that the 2PL model is able to reproduce the main item-level patterns of the observed data.

```{r ppc-2pl, eval=run_2pl}
p_hat_2pl <- matrix(NA, nrow = N, ncol = J)

for(j in 1:J) {
  p_hat_2pl[, j] <- inv_logit(a_hat_2pl[j] * (theta_hat_2pl - b_hat_2pl[j]))
}

Y_rep_2pl <- matrix(
  rbinom(N * J, size = 1, prob = as.vector(p_hat_2pl)),
  nrow = N,
  ncol = J
)

replicated_student_score_2pl <- rowMeans(Y_rep_2pl)
expected_student_score_2pl <- rowMeans(p_hat_2pl)

ppc_students_2pl <- data.frame(
  observed = observed_student_score,
  expected_prediction = expected_student_score_2pl,
  replicated = replicated_student_score_2pl
)

ppc_items_2pl <- data.frame(
  item = colnames(Y),
  observed_proportion = colMeans(Y),
  predicted_probability = colMeans(p_hat_2pl),
  replicated_proportion = colMeans(Y_rep_2pl)
)

cat("Summary statistics of observed student scores:\n")
cat("These values come from the real INVALSI response matrix.\n")
print(summary(observed_student_score))

cat("\nSummary statistics of replicated student scores from the 2PL model:\n")
cat("These values come from fake data simulated using the fitted 2PL model.\n")
print(summary(replicated_student_score_2pl))

cat("\nItem-level posterior predictive check for the 2PL model:\n")
cat("Observed proportions are compared with predicted probabilities and replicated proportions.\n")
print(ppc_items_2pl)
```


```{r ppc-2pl-students, eval=run_2pl}
ggplot(ppc_students_2pl, aes(x = observed, y = expected_prediction)) +
  geom_point(alpha = 0.4) +
  geom_abline(intercept = 0, slope = 1, linetype = "dashed") +
  labs(
    title = "Posterior predictive check: student scores, 2PL model",
    x = "Observed proportion correct",
    y = "Expected predicted proportion correct"
  )
```

**Interpretation of the 2PL student posterior predictive scatter plot.**  
Each point represents one student. The x-axis shows the observed proportion of correct answers obtained by the student, while the y-axis shows the expected proportion of correct answers predicted by the fitted 2PL model.

The dashed diagonal line represents perfect agreement between observed and predicted performance. Points close to this line indicate that the model predicts student performance well. A clear positive pattern is expected because students with higher observed scores should also receive higher predicted scores from the model.

This graph is useful because it checks the model at the student level, not only at the item level or in the general histogram. Compared with the Rasch model, the 2PL model can also use item discrimination, so items that separate students more strongly can have more influence on the estimated ability and on the predicted student score.

If the points are close to the diagonal line, the 2PL model reproduces the observed student scores well. If some points are systematically above the line, the model is overpredicting those students' performance. If some points are systematically below the line, the model is underpredicting their performance.

```{r ppc-2pl-hist, eval=run_2pl}
df_ppc_students_2pl <- data.frame(
  score = c(observed_student_score, replicated_student_score_2pl),
  data_type = rep(c("Observed data", "Replicated data from 2PL"), each = N)
)

ggplot(df_ppc_students_2pl, aes(x = score, fill = data_type)) +
  geom_histogram(alpha = 0.5, position = "identity", bins = 98) +
  labs(
    title = "Observed vs replicated student scores, 2PL model",
    x = "Proportion correct",
    y = "Number of students"
  )
```

**Interpretation of the 2PL posterior predictive histogram.**  
This histogram compares the real distribution of student scores with a replicated distribution simulated from the fitted 2PL model. If the two histograms overlap well, the model is able to reproduce the general level and spread of student performance. If the replicated distribution is very different from the observed one, this would suggest that the model is missing some important part of the response structure.

If the observed and replicated histograms overlap well, this suggests that the 2PL model is able to reproduce the overall distribution of student performance. This means that, after estimating ability, difficulty, and discrimination, the model can generate artificial data with a similar structure to the real data.

Small differences between the two histograms are normal because the replicated data are simulated and therefore contain random variation. However, large differences would suggest that the model is missing some important feature of the observed responses. As in the Rasch model, this graph should be interpreted as a global fit check: it tells us whether the model reproduces the general distribution of scores, but it does not prove that every single item is perfectly fitted.

In any case, this histograms looks look enough for our porpuses.

```{r ppc-2pl-items, eval=run_2pl, fig.width=10, fig.height=7}
ggplot(ppc_items_2pl, aes(x = observed_proportion, y = predicted_probability, label = item)) +
  geom_point(size = 2) +
  geom_abline(intercept = 0, slope = 1, linetype = "dashed") +
  geom_label_repel(
    size = 3,
    max.overlaps = Inf,
    box.padding = 0.35,
    point.padding = 0.25,
    min.segment.length = 0,
    segment.size = 0.3
  ) +
  labs(
    title = "Observed vs predicted item performance, 2PL model",
    x = "Observed proportion correct",
    y = "Predicted probability correct"
  ) +
  coord_cartesian(clip = "off") +
  theme(
    plot.margin = margin(10, 30, 10, 10)
  )
```

**Interpretation of the 2PL item posterior predictive scatter plot.**  
Each point represents one item. The x-axis shows the observed proportion of students who answered that item correctly, while the y-axis shows the average predicted probability of a correct answer according to the fitted 2PL model. The dashed diagonal line represents perfect agreement between observed and predicted item performance. Points close to the line mean that the 2PL model reproduces the observed item performance well.

Overall, if the points are close to the diagonal line, the 2PL model reproduces item-level performance well. This means that the average predicted probability for each item is close to the observed proportion of correct answers. Items far from the diagonal line would be more problematic, because they would indicate that the model overpredicts or underpredicts the performance on those specific items.

This check is important because a model can fit student scores reasonably well but still have problems with some individual items. For this reason, the item-level posterior predictive check complements the student-level predictive check and gives a more detailed view of model fit.

```{r ppc-2pl-predicted-vs-replicated, eval=run_2pl, fig.width=10, fig.height=7}
ppc_items_2pl <- ppc_items_2pl %>%
  mutate(
    replication_error = replicated_proportion - predicted_probability,
    abs_replication_error = abs(replication_error)
  )

ggplot(
  ppc_items_2pl,
  aes(
    x = predicted_probability,
    y = replicated_proportion,
    label = item
  )
) +
  geom_point(size = 2) +
  geom_abline(intercept = 0, slope = 1, linetype = "dashed") +
  geom_label_repel(
    size = 3,
    max.overlaps = Inf,
    box.padding = 0.35,
    point.padding = 0.25,
    min.segment.length = 0,
    segment.size = 0.3
  ) +
  labs(
    title = "Predicted vs replicated item performance, 2PL model",
    x = "Predicted probability correct",
    y = "Replicated proportion correct"
  ) +
  coord_cartesian(clip = "off") +
  theme(
    plot.margin = margin(10, 30, 10, 10)
  )
```

**Predicted probability vs replicated proportion.**  
This graph compares the model's expected probability of a correct answer with the proportion of correct answers obtained in one replicated dataset generated from the fitted 2PL model. Points close to the dashed diagonal line indicate that the simulated item proportions are coherent with the probabilities predicted by the model. Small deviations from the diagonal line are normal, because `replicated_proportion` is based on simulated 0/1 responses and therefore includes random simulation noise. For this reason, the graph should not be interpreted as requiring perfect equality between predicted and replicated values. Instead, it checks whether the replicated data behave in a way that is broadly consistent with the model predictions.

# Predictive comparison: 1PL vs 2PL

```{r predictive-comparison, eval=run_2pl}
p2_safe <- clip_probs(p_hat_2pl)

log_loss_2pl <- -mean(
  Y * log(p2_safe) + (1 - Y) * log(1 - p2_safe)
)

accuracy_2pl <- mean((p_hat_2pl > 0.5) == Y)

student_rmse_2pl <- rmse(observed_student_score, expected_student_score_2pl)
student_mae_2pl <- mae(observed_student_score, expected_student_score_2pl)

student_correlation_2pl <- cor(
  observed_student_score,
  expected_student_score_2pl,
  use = "complete.obs"
)

expected_item_score_2pl <- colMeans(p_hat_2pl)

item_rmse_2pl <- rmse(observed_item_score, expected_item_score_2pl)
item_mae_2pl <- mae(observed_item_score, expected_item_score_2pl)

item_correlation_2pl <- cor(
  observed_item_score,
  expected_item_score_2pl,
  use = "complete.obs"
)

indices_2pl <- data.frame(
  model = "2PL",
  log_loss = log_loss_2pl,
  accuracy = accuracy_2pl,
  student_rmse = student_rmse_2pl,
  student_mae = student_mae_2pl,
  student_correlation = student_correlation_2pl,
  item_rmse = item_rmse_2pl,
  item_mae = item_mae_2pl,
  item_correlation = item_correlation_2pl
)

predictive_comparison <- rbind(
  indices_1pl,
  indices_2pl
)

cat("Predictive comparison between the Rasch / 1PL model and the 2PL model:\n")
cat("Lower log_loss, RMSE, and MAE are better. Higher accuracy, student_correlation, and item_correlation are better.\n")
print(predictive_comparison)
```

# AIC and BIC comparison

**AIC and BIC.**  
The **Akaike Information Criterion (AIC)** and the **Bayesian Information Criterion (BIC)** are classical model comparison criteria based on the log-likelihood. Both criteria reward models that fit the data well, but they also penalize models with more parameters, so that a model is not preferred only because it is more flexible. Their formulas are:

\[
AIC = -2\log L(\hat{\theta}) + 2k
\]

\[
BIC = -2\log L(\hat{\theta}) + k\log(n)
\]

where \(\log L(\hat{\theta})\) is the log-likelihood evaluated at the estimated parameters, \(k\) is the number of estimated parameters, and \(n\) is the number of observed responses. Lower AIC or BIC values indicate a better trade-off between model fit and model complexity. In this project, these values are computed as approximate frequentist-style indicators, because the models are Bayesian and the log-likelihood is evaluated at the posterior mean parameters rather than at maximum likelihood estimates.

```{r aic-bic-comparison, eval=run_2pl}
extract_posterior_mean <- function(samples, par_name) {
  samples_matrix <- as.matrix(samples)
  selected_columns <- grep(
    paste0("^", par_name, "\\["),
    colnames(samples_matrix),
    value = TRUE
  )
  
  selected_columns <- selected_columns[
    order(as.numeric(gsub(paste0(par_name, "\\[|\\]"), "", selected_columns)))
  ]
  
  colMeans(samples_matrix[, selected_columns, drop = FALSE])
}

log_likelihood_1pl <- function(Y, theta, b) {
  p <- plogis(outer(theta, b, "-"))
  p <- pmin(pmax(p, 1e-10), 1 - 1e-10)
  
  sum(
    Y * log(p) + (1 - Y) * log(1 - p),
    na.rm = TRUE
  )
}

log_likelihood_2pl <- function(Y, theta, b, a) {
  eta <- sweep(outer(theta, b, "-"), 2, a, "*")
  p <- plogis(eta)
  p <- pmin(pmax(p, 1e-10), 1 - 1e-10)
  
  sum(
    Y * log(p) + (1 - Y) * log(1 - p),
    na.rm = TRUE
  )
}

Y_ic <- Y
n_observed <- sum(!is.na(Y_ic))

theta_hat_1pl_ic <- extract_posterior_mean(samples_rasch, "theta")
b_hat_1pl_ic <- extract_posterior_mean(samples_rasch, "b")

theta_hat_2pl_ic <- extract_posterior_mean(samples_2pl, "theta")
b_hat_2pl_ic <- extract_posterior_mean(samples_2pl, "b")
a_hat_2pl_ic <- extract_posterior_mean(samples_2pl, "a")

ll_1pl <- log_likelihood_1pl(Y_ic, theta_hat_1pl_ic, b_hat_1pl_ic)
ll_2pl <- log_likelihood_2pl(Y_ic, theta_hat_2pl_ic, b_hat_2pl_ic, a_hat_2pl_ic)

k_1pl <- length(theta_hat_1pl_ic) + length(b_hat_1pl_ic)
k_2pl <- length(theta_hat_2pl_ic) + length(b_hat_2pl_ic) + length(a_hat_2pl_ic)

aic_bic_results <- data.frame(
  model = c("1PL / Rasch", "2PL"),
  log_likelihood = c(ll_1pl, ll_2pl),
  number_parameters = c(k_1pl, k_2pl),
  n_observed = c(n_observed, n_observed),
  AIC = c(
    -2 * ll_1pl + 2 * k_1pl,
    -2 * ll_2pl + 2 * k_2pl
  ),
  BIC = c(
    -2 * ll_1pl + log(n_observed) * k_1pl,
    -2 * ll_2pl + log(n_observed) * k_2pl
  )
)

aic_bic_results %>%
  knitr::kable(
    digits = 3,
    caption = "Approximate AIC and BIC comparison between the 1PL/Rasch and 2PL models"
  )
```

**Interpretation of AIC and BIC.**  
AIC and BIC provide an additional comparison between the Rasch / 1PL model and the 2PL model. If the 2PL model has lower AIC or BIC, this means that its better fit is large enough to compensate for the additional discrimination parameters. If the values are similar, or if the Rasch model has lower values, then the simpler model may be preferable. However, in this Bayesian project these indicators should be interpreted only as approximate support, because DIC is more directly connected to the MCMC posterior distribution.

In our case for both indicators 2pl is better.

# DIC comparison

**Deviance Information Criterion.**  
The **Deviance Information Criterion (DIC)** is a Bayesian model comparison criterion designed for models estimated with MCMC methods. It is based on the **deviance**, which measures lack of fit: lower deviance means better fit to the observed data. At the same time, DIC penalizes model complexity through an effective number of parameters. The formula is:

\[
DIC = \bar{D}(\theta) + p_D = 2\bar{D}(\theta) - D(\bar{\theta})
\]

where:

\[
D(\theta) = -2\log p(y \mid \theta) + C
\]

\(\bar{D}(\theta)\) is the posterior mean deviance,
\(D(\bar{\theta})\) is the deviance evaluated at the posterior mean parameters, and
\(p_D = \bar{D}(\theta) - D(\bar{\theta})\) is the effective number of parameters. Therefore, DIC rewards good fit but penalizes excessive complexity. A lower DIC usually indicates a better balance between model fit and model complexity.

In this project, DIC is especially useful because the 1PL/Rasch and 2PL models are Bayesian models estimated through MCMC. Compared with AIC and BIC, DIC is more appropriate here because it uses the posterior distribution and an effective complexity penalty, instead of relying only on maximum likelihood estimates and a fixed number of parameters. For this reason, DIC is the main information criterion used to compare the simpler Rasch model with the more flexible 2PL model.

DIC computation can be slower than AIC and BIC because it is based on posterior deviance information. In reuse mode, the saved DIC results are loaded and not recomputed.

```{r dic-comparison, eval=run_dic}
if(reuse_existing_results) {
  load(file.path(results_dir, "dic_results.RData"), envir = .GlobalEnv)
  cat("Reuse mode: DIC results loaded from dic_results.RData. They are not recomputed.\n\n")
}

cat("DIC for the Rasch / 1PL model:\n")
cat("Lower DIC usually indicates a better trade-off between fit and complexity.\n")
print(dic_rasch)

dic_comparison <- extract_dic_values(dic_rasch, "Rasch / 1PL")

if(run_2pl && exists("dic_2pl")) {
  cat("\nDIC for the 2PL model:\n")
  cat("This can be compared with the Rasch DIC. Lower DIC is usually preferred.\n")
  print(dic_2pl)

  dic_comparison <- rbind(
    dic_comparison,
    extract_dic_values(dic_2pl, "2PL")
  )
}

cat("\nSimple DIC comparison table:\n")
print(dic_comparison)
```

**Interpretation of the DIC table.**  
The DIC table compares the two Bayesian IRT models by combining model fit and effective complexity. The 2PL model is more flexible because it estimates both item difficulty and item discrimination, while the Rasch model estimates only item difficulty. Therefore, the 2PL model should be preferred only if its improvement in fit is large enough to compensate for this additional complexity. If the DIC difference is small, the evidence in favour of one model is weak and the simpler Rasch model can still be considered competitive.

## Deviance trace for DIC stability

The DIC is computed from the posterior deviance. For this reason, I also inspect the MCMC trace of the total deviance. This is not exactly a trace of the DIC itself, because DIC is a final summary value, but it is a useful check of the quantity on which the DIC is based. If the deviance trace fluctuates around a stable level and the chains overlap well, the DIC estimate is more reliable. If the deviance trace has strong trends or separated chains, the DIC comparison should be interpreted with caution.

```{r deviance-trace-rasch, eval=run_dic, fig.width=9, fig.height=5}
if(exists("deviance_trace_rasch")) {
  cat("Trace of total deviance for the Rasch / 1PL model:\n")
  traceplot(deviance_trace_rasch)
  
  cat("\nSummary of total deviance for the Rasch / 1PL model:\n")
  print(summary(deviance_trace_rasch))
  
  deviance_trace_summary_rasch <- summarise_deviance_trace(
    deviance_trace_rasch,
    "Rasch / 1PL"
  )
  
  cat("\nCompact summary of total deviance for the Rasch / 1PL model:\n")
  print(deviance_trace_summary_rasch)
} else {
  cat(
    "The Rasch deviance trace is not available. ",
    "Run the project once in fast or final mode with run_dic = TRUE, ",
    "then reuse mode will be able to load it.\n"
  )
}
```

The traceplot above checks whether the total deviance of the Rasch / 1PL model is stable across the MCMC iterations. A stable trace should move around a constant level without long trends. This matters because the DIC uses the posterior deviance, so an unstable deviance chain would make the final DIC value less trustworthy.

```{r deviance-trace-2pl, eval=run_dic && run_2pl, fig.width=9, fig.height=5}
if(exists("deviance_trace_2pl")) {
  cat("Trace of total deviance for the 2PL model:\n")
  traceplot(deviance_trace_2pl)
  
  cat("\nSummary of total deviance for the 2PL model:\n")
  print(summary(deviance_trace_2pl))
  
  deviance_trace_summary_2pl <- summarise_deviance_trace(
    deviance_trace_2pl,
    "2PL"
  )
  
  cat("\nCompact summary of total deviance for the 2PL model:\n")
  print(deviance_trace_summary_2pl)
} else {
  cat(
    "The 2PL deviance trace is not available. ",
    "Run the project once in fast or final mode with run_dic = TRUE, ",
    "then reuse mode will be able to load it.\n"
  )
}
```

The traceplot above performs the same check for the 2PL model. Since the 2PL model is more flexible and has more parameters, checking the deviance trace is especially useful before interpreting its DIC. If the deviance trace is stable, the DIC comparison between Rasch / 1PL and 2PL is more credible.

```{r deviance-trace-comparison, eval=run_dic}
dev_trace_tables <- list()

if(exists("deviance_trace_summary_rasch")) {
  dev_trace_tables[[length(dev_trace_tables) + 1]] <- deviance_trace_summary_rasch
}

if(exists("deviance_trace_summary_2pl")) {
  dev_trace_tables[[length(dev_trace_tables) + 1]] <- deviance_trace_summary_2pl
}

if(length(dev_trace_tables) > 0) {
  deviance_trace_comparison <- do.call(rbind, dev_trace_tables)
  
  cat("Comparison of total deviance traces:\n")
  print(deviance_trace_comparison)
} else {
  cat("No deviance trace summaries are available yet.\n")
}
```

# Save results

```{r save-results}
if(reuse_existing_results) {

  cat("Reuse mode is active. Existing result files were loaded and not overwritten.\n")
  cat("The simulation settings were retrieved from run_metadata.RData.\n")

} else {

  run_metadata <- list(
    run_mode = run_mode,
    n_sample = N,
    n_items = J,
    n_chains = n_chains,
    n_adapt = n_adapt,
    burn_in = burn_in,
    n_iter = n_iter,
    thin_value = thin_value,
    run_2pl = run_2pl,
    run_dic = run_dic,
    data_path = data_path,
    results_dir = normalizePath(results_dir, mustWork = FALSE),
    saved_at = Sys.time()
  )

  write.csv(results_students_1pl, file.path(results_dir, "results_students_1pl.csv"), row.names = FALSE)
  write.csv(results_items_1pl, file.path(results_dir, "results_items_1pl.csv"), row.names = FALSE)

  if(run_2pl) {
    write.csv(results_students_2pl, file.path(results_dir, "results_students_2pl.csv"), row.names = FALSE)
    write.csv(results_items_2pl, file.path(results_dir, "results_items_2pl.csv"), row.names = FALSE)
    write.csv(predictive_comparison, file.path(results_dir, "predictive_comparison.csv"), row.names = FALSE)
    
    if(exists("aic_bic_results")) {
      write.csv(aic_bic_results, file.path(results_dir, "aic_bic_results.csv"), row.names = FALSE)
    }
  }

  rasch_objects_to_save <- c(
    "samples_rasch",
    "results_students_1pl",
    "results_items_1pl",
    "indices_1pl"
  )

  if(run_dic && exists("dic_rasch")) {
    rasch_objects_to_save <- c(rasch_objects_to_save, "dic_rasch")
  }

  if(run_dic && exists("deviance_trace_rasch")) {
    rasch_objects_to_save <- c(rasch_objects_to_save, "deviance_trace_rasch")
  }

  save(
    list = rasch_objects_to_save,
    file = file.path(results_dir, "rasch_results.RData")
  )

  if(run_2pl) {

    two_pl_objects_to_save <- c(
      "samples_2pl",
      "results_students_2pl",
      "results_items_2pl",
      "predictive_comparison"
    )
    
    if(exists("aic_bic_results")) {
      two_pl_objects_to_save <- c(two_pl_objects_to_save, "aic_bic_results")
    }

    if(run_dic && exists("dic_2pl")) {
      two_pl_objects_to_save <- c(two_pl_objects_to_save, "dic_2pl")
    }

    if(run_dic && exists("deviance_trace_2pl")) {
      two_pl_objects_to_save <- c(two_pl_objects_to_save, "deviance_trace_2pl")
    }

    save(
      list = two_pl_objects_to_save,
      file = file.path(results_dir, "two_model_comparison_results.RData")
    )
  }

  if(run_dic && exists("dic_comparison")) {

    dic_objects_to_save <- c("dic_rasch", "dic_comparison")

    if(exists("dic_2pl")) {
      dic_objects_to_save <- c(dic_objects_to_save, "dic_2pl")
    }

    if(exists("deviance_trace_rasch")) {
      dic_objects_to_save <- c(dic_objects_to_save, "deviance_trace_rasch")
    }

    if(exists("deviance_trace_2pl")) {
      dic_objects_to_save <- c(dic_objects_to_save, "deviance_trace_2pl")
    }

    if(exists("deviance_trace_comparison")) {
      dic_objects_to_save <- c(dic_objects_to_save, "deviance_trace_comparison")
    }

    save(
      list = dic_objects_to_save,
      file = file.path(results_dir, "dic_results.RData")
    )

    write.csv(dic_comparison, file.path(results_dir, "dic_comparison.csv"), row.names = FALSE)

    if(exists("deviance_trace_comparison")) {
      write.csv(
        deviance_trace_comparison,
        file.path(results_dir, "deviance_trace_comparison.csv"),
        row.names = FALSE
      )
    }
  }

  save(
    run_metadata,
    file = file.path(results_dir, "run_metadata.RData")
  )

  cat("Files saved in the current working directory:\n")
  cat("- sampled_data.RData\n")
  cat("- run_metadata.RData\n")
  cat("- results_students_1pl.csv\n")
  cat("- results_items_1pl.csv\n")
  cat("- rasch_results.RData\n")

  if(run_2pl) {
    cat("- results_students_2pl.csv\n")
    cat("- results_items_2pl.csv\n")
    cat("- predictive_comparison.csv\n")
    cat("- two_model_comparison_results.RData\n")
  }

  if(run_dic && exists("dic_comparison")) {
    cat("- dic_results.RData\n")
    cat("- dic_comparison.csv\n")
  }
}
```

# Final results to discuss

Some important results to discuss:

```{r final-objects}
cat("Main Rasch / 1PL validation results:\n")
cat("Correlation between Rasch ability and WLE_ITA_200:\n")
print(cor_theta1_wle)

cat("\nCorrelation between Rasch ability and raw score:\n")
print(cor_theta1_raw)

cat("\nCorrelation between Rasch item difficulty and observed item proportion correct:\n")
print(cor_b1_itemscore)

cat("\nPredictive indices for the Rasch / 1PL model:\n")
print(indices_1pl)

if(run_2pl) {
  cat("\nMain 2PL validation and comparison results:\n")

  cat("\nCorrelation between 2PL ability and WLE_ITA_200:\n")
  print(cor_theta2_wle)

  cat("\nCorrelation between 2PL ability and raw score:\n")
  print(cor_theta2_raw)

  cat("\nCorrelation between 1PL and 2PL ability estimates:\n")
  print(cor_theta_1pl_2pl)

  cat("\nCorrelation between 1PL and 2PL item difficulty estimates:\n")
  print(cor_b_1pl_2pl)

  cat("\nPredictive comparison between 1PL and 2PL:\n")
  print(predictive_comparison)
}
```

# Conclusion

The Rasch model estimates student ability and item difficulty using a simple and interpretable Bayesian IRT structure.

The 2PL model extends the Rasch model by adding item discrimination. This allows some items to be more informative than others.

The comparison with `WLE_ITA_200` is useful because it checks whether the Bayesian ability estimates move in the same direction as the official INVALSI score.

The posterior predictive checks and predictive indices help evaluate whether the models reproduce the observed response matrix well.

If the 2PL model has lower log-loss, better predictive indices, and meaningful discrimination parameters, then it provides more information than the simpler Rasch model. If the results are very similar, then the Rasch model may be preferred because it is simpler and easier to interpret.

Using dic as well, we can however notice that 2pl is a lot better.
